original: Maðr er nefndr Ægir eða Hlér. Hann bjó í ey þeiri, er nú er kölluð Hlésey. Hann var mjög fjölkunnigr.

translations.faulkes: A man was named Aegir or Hler. He lived on that island which is now called Hlesey. He was very skilled in magic.
translations.brodeur: A certain man was named Ægir or Hlér. He dwelt on the island which is now called Hlér's Isle; he was very skilled in magic.
translations.literal: Man is named Aegir or Hler. He dwelt in island that, which now is called Hlesey. He was much magic-skilled.

original: Hann fór til Ásgarðs. En er æsir vissu ferð hans, var honum vel fagnað, en þó var mörgu snúit með sjónhverfingum.

translations.faulkes: He set out for Asgard. When the Aesir knew of his journey, he was well received, but many things were nevertheless turned into visual delusions.
translations.brodeur: He fared to Asgard... when the Aesir knew of his journey, he was right well received; yet many things were done with visual delusions.
translations.literal: He went to Asgard. But when Aesir knew journey his, was him well welcomed, but though was much turned with sight-delusions.

original: Um kveldið, er drukkið skyldi, þá lét Óðinn bera inn í höllina sverð, ok var þar svá mikit lýsi, at ekki þurfti annað ljós.

translations.faulkes: In the evening, when they were to drink, Odin had swords brought into the hall, and there was such a brightness from them that no other light was needed.
translations.brodeur: At evening, when it was time for drinking, Odin had swords brought into the hall, and they were so bright that no other light was needed.
translations.literal: Around evening-the, when drunk should [be], then let Odin bear in in hall-the swords, and was there so much light, that not needed other light.

original: Þá gengu æsir til borðs ok settust í hásæti... Óðinn, Þórr, Njörðr, Freyr, Týr, Heimdallr, Bragi, Víðarr, Váli, Ullr, Hænir, Forseti, Loki.

translations.faulkes: Then the Aesir went to the table and sat in the high seats... Odin, Thor, Njord, Freyr, Tyr, Heimdall, Bragi, Vidar, Vali, Ullr, Hoenir, Forseti, Loki.
translations.brodeur: Then the Aesir sat them down to the banquet... Odin, Thor, Njördr, Freyr, Týr, Heimdallr, Bragi, Víðarr, Váli, Ullr, Hönir, Forseti, Loki.
translations.literal: Then went Aesir to board [table] and sat-themselves in high-seats... [List of Deities].'

original: Svá ok ásynjur: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Iðunn, Gerðr, Sigyn, Fulla, Nanna.

translations.faulkes: So also the goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Idunn, Gerd, Sigyn, Fulla, Nanna.
translations.brodeur: And likewise the Goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Idunn, Gerdr, Sigyn, Fulla, Nanna.
translations.literal: So also goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Idunn, Gerd, Sigyn, Fulla, Nanna.

original: Ægir sat næst Braga. Þeir tókust á ræðum, ok sagði Bragi Ægi mörg tíðindi.

translations.faulkes: Aegir sat next to Bragi. They engaged in conversation, and Bragi told Aegir many things that had happened.
translations.brodeur: Ægir sat next to Bragi; they took part in conversation and drinking; and Bragi told Ægir of many things which had come to pass.
translations.literal: Aegir sat next Bragi. They took-each-other on talk [conversed], and said Bragi Aegir many tidings.

original: Bragi sagði frá því, er Þjazi jötunn tók Iðunni ok eplin.

translations.faulkes: Bragi told how the giant Thjazi took Idunn and the apples.
translations.brodeur: Bragi told how the giant Thjazi took Idunn and her apples.
translations.literal: Bragi said from that, when Thjazi giant took Idunn and apples-the.

original: Óðinn ok Lóðurr ok Hænir fóru heiman ok gengu um fjöll ok eyðimerkur.

translations.faulkes: Odin and Lodur and Hoenir set out from home and traveled over mountains and wilderness.
translations.brodeur: Odin and Lóðurr and Hönir went forth from home and were wandering over mountains and wastes.
translations.literal: Odin and Lodur and Hoenir went from-home and went around mountains and wildernesses.

original: Þeir kómu í dal einn ok sáu þar uxaflokk. Þeir tóku einn uxa ok suðu í katli.

translations.faulkes: They came into a valley and saw a herd of oxen there. They took one ox and boiled it in a pot.
translations.brodeur: They came into a certain dale and saw a herd of oxen; they took one ox and boiled it in a cauldron.
translations.literal: They came in valley one and saw there ox-flock. They took one ox and boiled in kettle.

original: En er þeir lauku upp katlinum, þá var ekki soðit. Þat bar við í annat sinn, ok var þó eigi soðit.

translations.faulkes: But when they opened the pot, it was not cooked. This happened a second time, and still it was not cooked.
translations.brodeur: But when they opened the cauldron, it was not cooked... it happened a second time, and still it was not cooked.
translations.literal: But when they locked up [opened] kettle-the, then was not cooked. That happened at second time, and was though not cooked.

original: Örn einn sat í eikinni yfir þeim ok mælti: 'Ef þér viljið gefa mér fylli mína af oxanum, þá mun soðna í katlinum.'

translations.faulkes: An eagle sat in the oak above them and said: 'If you will give me my fill of the ox, then it will cook in the pot.'
translations.brodeur: A certain eagle sat in the oak-tree above them, and spake: 'If ye will give me my fill of the ox, then it shall be cooked in the cauldron.'
translations.literal: Eagle one sat in oak-the over them and spoke: 'If you will give me fill mine of ox-the, then will [it] cook in kettle-the.'

original: Þeir játtu því. Þá lét hann sígask ór trénu ok settist á seyðinn ok tók þegar lærin tvö ok báða bóguna.

translations.faulkes: They agreed to this. Then it let itself down from the tree and sat on the meat and immediately took the two hams and both shoulders.
translations.brodeur: They said yes to him. Then he let himself sink out of the tree and sat down by the fire, and straightway took the two hams and both shoulders.
translations.literal: They agreed that. Then let he [himself] sink from tree-the and sat-himself on meat-the and took at-once hams-the two and both shoulders-the.

original: Þá varð Loki reiðr ok tók stang eina mikla ok laust í kviðinn örninum.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki became angry and took a large pole and struck the eagle in the belly.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki became wroth, and took a great pole and struck with it at the eagle's belly.
translations.literal: Then became Loki angry and took pole one great and struck in belly-the eagle-the.

original: Örninn flaug upp, en stöngin fastnaði við kviðinn ok hendr Loka við annan endann.

translations.faulkes: The eagle flew up, and the pole stuck to its belly and Loki's hands to the other end.
translations.brodeur: The eagle flew up, and the pole was fast to the eagle's belly, and Loki's hands to the other end of the pole.
translations.literal: Eagle-the flew up, but pole-the fastened with belly-the and hands Loki's with other end-the.

original: Loki hljóp með fótunum við grjót ok skóga, ok ætlaði hann, at handleggir hans mundu slitna.

translations.faulkes: Loki's feet struck against rocks and trees, and he thought his arms would be torn off.
translations.brodeur: Loki's feet were dragged against stones and rock-heaps and woods, and he thought his arms would be torn from his shoulders.
translations.literal: Loki leaped [was dragged] with feet-the with stones and forests, and intended [thought] he, that arms his would tear-off.

original: Örninn mælti, at Loki skyldi aldri lauss verða, nema hann kæmi Iðunni út ór Ásgarði með eplin.

translations.faulkes: The eagle said that Loki would never be released unless he brought Idunn out of Asgard with her apples.
translations.brodeur: The eagle said that Loki should never be loosed, unless he would give him his oath to bring Idunn out of Asgard with her apples.
translations.literal: Eagle-the spoke, that Loki should never loose become, unless he came [brought] Idunn out from Asgard with apples-the.

original: Loki lofaði því ok varð lauss ok fór til félaga sinna.

translations.faulkes: Loki promised this and was released and went to his companions.
translations.brodeur: Loki promised it, and becoming free, went to his companions.
translations.literal: Loki promised that and became loose and went to companions his.

original: At ákveðnum tíma teygði Loki Iðunni út ór Ásgarði í skóg nokkurn.

translations.faulkes: At the appointed time Loki lured Idunn out of Asgard into a certain forest.
translations.brodeur: At the appointed time Loki lured Idunn out of Asgard into a certain wood.
translations.literal: At appointed time lured Loki Idunn out from Asgard in forest some.

original: Þá kom Þjazi jötunn í arnarfham ok tók Iðunni ok flaug með hana til Þrymheims.

translations.faulkes: Then the giant Thjazi came in eagle-form and took Idunn and flew with her to Thrymheim.
translations.brodeur: Then the giant Thjazi came in his eagle's plumage and took Idunn and flew away with her to Thrymheimr.
translations.literal: Then came Thjazi giant in eagle-hide and took Idunn and flew with her to Thrymheim.

original: En æsir urðu illa við, er Iðunn var horfin, ok urðu þeir brátt hárir ok gamlir.

translations.faulkes: But the Aesir were in a bad way when Idunn disappeared, and they soon became grey-haired and old.
translations.brodeur: But the Aesir were ill-content at the disappearance of Idunn, and they became straightway hoary and old.
translations.literal: But Aesir became ill-with [distressed], when Idunn was vanished, and became they soon grey and old.

original: Söfnuðust æsirnir á þing ok spurðu, hverr síðast vissi til Iðunnar. En þat var síðast sét, at hón gekk út ór Ásgarði með Loka.

translations.faulkes: The Aesir gathered at the assembly and asked who had last seen Idunn. And she had last been seen going out of Asgard with Loki.
translations.brodeur: The Aesir assembled in council, and asked one another who had last seen Idunn; and it was seen last that she went out of Asgard with Loki.
translations.literal: Gathered Aesir-the on assembly and asked, who last knew to Idunn. But that was last seen, that she went out from Asgard with Loki.

original: Þá var Loki tekinn ok færðr á þingið, ok var honum hótað bana eða píslum. Hann varð hræddr ok hét at sækja Iðunni í Jötunheima.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki was seized and brought to the assembly, and he was threatened with death or torture. He became afraid and promised to fetch Idunn from Jotunheim.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki was seized and brought to the Thing, and he was threatened with death or with torture. He became adread, and said that he would seek after Idunn in Jötunheim.
translations.literal: Then was Loki taken and brought on assembly-the, and was him threatened death or tortures. He became afraid and promised to seek Idunn in Giant-home.

original: Hann fékk valsham hjá Freyju ok flaug í Jötunheima. Hann kom til Þjaza jötuns, en hann var róinn á sjá.

translations.faulkes: He got a falcon-shape from Freyja and flew to Jotunheim. He came to the giant Thjazi's, but he had rowed out to sea.
translations.brodeur: He got the falcon-shape which Freyja owned, and flew into Jötunheim... he came to the giant Thjazi's, but the giant had rowed out to sea.
translations.literal: He got falcon-hide with Freyja and flew in Giant-home. He came to Thjazi giant, but he was rowed on sea.

original: Loki tók Iðunni ok brá henni í hnotar líki ok hafði í klóm sér ok flaug sem mest mátti hann.

translations.faulkes: Loki took Idunn and changed her into the shape of a nut and held her in his claws and flew as hard as he could.
translations.brodeur: Loki took Idunn and changed her into the likeness of a nut, and held her in his claws and flew with all his might.
translations.literal: Loki took Idunn and cast her in nut's likeness and had in claws hers [his] and flew as most might he.

original: En er Þjazi kom heim ok saknaði Iðunni, þá tók hann arnarhaminn ok flaug eftir Loka.

translations.faulkes: When Thjazi came home and missed Idunn, he took his eagle-form and flew after Loki.
translations.brodeur: But when Thjazi came home and missed Idunn, he took his eagle's plumage and flew after Loki.
translations.literal: But when Thjazi came home and missed Idunn, then took he eagle-hide-the and flew after Loki.

original: Æsirnir sáu, hvar valrinn flaug með hnotina ok örninn eftir. Þeir fóru út undir borgarvegginn ok höfðu með sér lokar af spánum.

translations.faulkes: The Aesir saw where the falcon was flying with the nut and the eagle after it. They went out to the castle wall and took with them bundles of wood-shavings.
translations.brodeur: The Aesir saw where the falcon flew with the nut, and the eagle after him... they went out under the castle-wall and bore thither bundles of plane-shavings.
translations.literal: Aesir-the saw, where falcon-the flew with nut-the and eagle-the after. They went out under burg-wall-the and had with them bundles of shavings.

original: Þegar er valrinn flaug inn yfir borgina, þá létu þeir eld í spánuna. Örninn mátti eigi stöðva sig ok kom í eldinn.

translations.faulkes: As soon as the falcon flew in over the castle, they set fire to the shavings. The eagle could not stop itself and flew into the fire.
translations.brodeur: As soon as the falcon flew in over the wall, they set fire to the shavings. The eagle could not stop himself... and fell within the castle.
translations.literal: When that falcon-the flew in over burg-the, then let they fire in shavings-the. Eagle-the might not stop himself and came in fire-the.

original: Svá brunnu fjaðrar hans, ok nam staðar fluginn. Þá váru æsirnir nær ok drápu Þjaza jötun innan borgarhliðs, ok er þat víg mjög ágætt.

translations.faulkes: So his feathers burned, and his flight stopped. Then the Aesir were nearby and killed the giant Thjazi inside the castle gate, and that killing is very famous.
translations.brodeur: So his feathers burned, and his flight ceased. Then the Aesir were near and slew Thjazi the giant within the castle-gate, and that slaying is exceeding famous.
translations.literal: So burned feathers his, and took stead [stopped] flight-the. Then were Aesir-the near and slew Thjazi giant within burg-gate, and is that slaying much excellent.

original: Skaði, dóttir Þjaza, tók hjálm ok brynju ok öll hervápn ok fór til Ásgarðs at hefna föður síns.

translations.faulkes: Skadi, daughter of Thjazi, took helmet and mail-shirt and all weapons of war and went to Asgard to avenge her father.
translations.brodeur: Skaði, the daughter of the giant Thjazi, took helm and birnie and all weapons of war and fared to Asgard to avenge her father.
translations.literal: Skadi, daughter of-Thjazi, took helmet and byrnie and all war-weapons and went to Asgard to avenge father hers.

original: En æsir buðu henni sætt ok bætr. Hit fyrsta, at hón skyldi kjósa sér mann ór hópi ásanna ok kjósa at fótum ok sjá ekki annat til.

translations.faulkes: But the Aesir offered her a settlement and compensation. First, she was to choose herself a husband from among the Aesir and choose by the feet and see nothing else of them.
translations.brodeur: But the Aesir offered her a settlement and atonement: the first item was that she should choose herself a husband from among the Aesir, and choose by the feet only, seeing no more of him.
translations.literal: But Aesir offered her settlement and compensation. The first, that she should choose for-herself man from group of-Aesir and choose at feet and see not other to.

original: Hón sá eins manns fætur harðla fagra ok mælti: 'Þenna kýs ek. Fátt mun ljótt á Baldri.' En þat var Njörðr ór Nóatúnum.

translations.faulkes: She saw one man's feet which were very beautiful and said: 'I choose this one. Little will be ugly about Baldr.' But it was Njord of Noatun.
translations.brodeur: She saw the feet of one man, which were exceeding fair, and said: 'This one I choose; few things are hideous about Baldr.' But it was Njördr of Nóatún.
translations.literal: She saw one man's feet very fair and spoke: 'This-one choose I. Few-things will-be ugly on Baldr.' But that was Njord from Noatun.

original: Þat hafði hón ok í sættinni, at æsir skyldu þat gera, er hón hugði, at þeir mundu eigi mega, at hlæja hana.

translations.faulkes: It was also in the settlement that the Aesir were to do what she thought they would not be able to do: make her laugh.
translations.brodeur: Another item of the settlement was that which she thought they could not perform: to make her laugh.
translations.literal: That had she also in settlement-the, that Aesir should that do, which she thought, that they would not may, to laugh her.

original: Þá gerði Loki þat, at hann batt um skegg geitar nokkurrar ok öðrum enda um hreðjar sér.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki did this: he tied one end of a cord to a certain goat's beard and the other end around his own testicles.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki did this: he tied a cord about the beard of a certain goat, and the other end about his own members.
translations.literal: Then made Loki that, that he bound around beard of-goat some and other end around testicles his-self.

original: Létu þau ymist eftir, ok skrækkti hvárt tveggja við hátt. Þá lét Loki fállast í kné Skaða, ok þá hló hón. Var þá sætt ger með þeim.

translations.faulkes: They took turns pulling, and both squealed loudly. Then Loki let himself fall into Skadi's lap, and then she laughed. Then the settlement with her was completed.
translations.brodeur: They pulled each in turn, and each squealed aloud; then Loki let himself fall onto Skaði's knee, and she laughed. Therewith the atonement was made with her on the part of the Aesir.
translations.literal: Let they each after [pulled against each other], and squealed each two with loud. Then let Loki fall-himself in knee Skadi, and then laughed she. Was then settlement made with them.

original: Óðinn gerði þat til yfirbóta henni, at hann tók augu Þjaza ok kastaði upp á himin ok gerði af tvær stjörnur.

translations.faulkes: Odin did this to compensate her: he took Thjazi's eyes and threw them up into the sky and made two stars out of them.
translations.brodeur: It is said that Odin, as further satisfaction, took Thjazi's eyes and cast them up into the heavens, and made of them two stars.
translations.literal: Odin made that to compensation her, that he took eyes of-Thjazi and threw up on heaven and made from-it two stars.

original: Þá mælti Ægir: 'Mikill virðist mér Þjazi fyrir sér. Hvers kyns var hann?'

translations.faulkes: Then Aegir said: 'Thjazi seems to me to have been very powerful. What was his ancestry?'
translations.brodeur: Then spake Ægir: 'Thjazi seems to me to have been very mighty; of what family was he?'
translations.literal: Then spoke Aegir: 'Great seems-to me Thjazi for himself. What kin was he?'

original: Bragi svarar: 'Faðir hans hét Ölvaldi, ok munu þér þykja merkilig, er ek segi þér frá honum.'

translations.faulkes: Bragi replied: 'His father was called Olvaldi, and you will find what I tell you about him remarkable.'
translations.brodeur: Bragi answered: 'His father was called Ölvaldi; and if I tell thee of him, thou wilt think it a wonder.'
translations.literal: Bragi answers: 'Father his named Olvaldi, and will you think remarkable, when I say to-you from him.'

original: Hann var mjög auðigr at gulli. En er hann dó, þá skyldu synir hans skipta arfi, Iði ok Gangr ok Þjazi.

translations.faulkes: He was very rich in gold. And when he died, his sons were to divide the inheritance, Idi and Gang and Thjazi.
translations.brodeur: He was very rich in gold; and when he died, his sons should share their heritage, Idi and Gangr and Thjazi.
translations.literal: He was much rich at gold. But when he died, then should sons his divide inheritance, Idi and Gang and Thjazi.

original: Þeir mældu gullit með munntali, at hverr tók munnfylli sína, ok svá allir jafnt.

translations.faulkes: They measured the gold by mouthfuls, each taking his mouthful, and all an equal number.
translations.brodeur: They measured the gold by mouthfuls, each taking his mouthful, and so all of them an equal number.
translations.literal: They measured gold-the with mouth-tale, that each took mouth-fill his, and so all equally.

original: Fyrir því köllum vér nú gullit munntal þessa jötna, en vér leynum því í rúnum eða í skáldskap, ok köllum vér þat mál eða orðtök þessa jötna.'

translations.faulkes: Because of this we now call gold the mouthful of these giants, but we conceal it in runes or in poetry, and we call it the speech or words of these giants.'
translations.brodeur: Therefore we now call gold the mouth-tale of these giants, but we conceal it in runes or in poetry, and we call it the speech or words of these giants.'
translations.literal: Because that call we now gold-the mouth-tale of-these giants, but we hide that in runes or in poetry, and call we that speech or words of-these giants.'

original: Guðin höfðu ósætt við þat fólk, er Vanir heita. En þeir lögðu með sér friðstefnu ok settu grið á þá lund, at þeir gengu báðir til eins kers ok spýttu í hráka sínum.

translations.faulkes: The gods had a dispute with the people called Vanir. They held a peace meeting and established a truce by both going to a vat and spitting their saliva into it.
translations.brodeur: The gods had a dispute with the people called Vanir... they appointed a peace-meeting and established a truce in this wise: both parties went to a vessel and let their spittle fall into it.
translations.literal: Gods-the had dispute with that folk, which Vanir named-are. And they laid between themselves peace-meeting and set truce on that wise, that they went both to one vat and spat in saliva theirs.

original: En at skilnaði þá tóku goðin ok vildu eigi láta týnast þat griðamark ok sköpuðu þar ór mann. Sá heitir Kvasir.

translations.faulkes: At their parting the gods took it and, not wanting that symbol of truce to be lost, they shaped a man from it. He is called Kvasir.
translations.brodeur: Then at parting, the gods took that truce-token and would not let it perish, but wrought thereof a man. This man is called Kvasir.
translations.literal: But at parting then took gods-the and would not let perish that truce-mark and shaped there from man. That-one is-named Kvasir.

original: Hann er svá vitr, at engi spyrr hann þeira hluta, er hann kann eigi úrlausn.

translations.faulkes: He is so wise that no one asks him any question to which he does not have the answer.
translations.brodeur: He is so wise that no man can ask him any question which he cannot answer.
translations.literal: He is so wise, that no-one asks him those things, which he knows not solution.

original: Hann fór víða um heim at kenna mönnum fræði. En er hann kom til dverga nokkurra, Fjalars ok Galars, þá kölluðu þeir hann á einmæli ok drápu hann.

translations.faulkes: He traveled far across the world to teach people knowledge. But when he came to certain dwarfs, Fjalar and Galar, they called him to a private meeting and killed him.
translations.brodeur: He fared far about the world to teach men knowledge; and on a time he came to certain dwarfs, Fjalar and Galar. They called him to a private council, and slew him.
translations.literal: He went widely around world to teach men knowledge. But when he came to dwarfs some, Fjalar and Galar, then called they him to one-talk [private] and slew him.

original: Létu þeir renna blóð hans í tvö ker ok einn ketil. Heitir ketillinn Óðrerir, en kerin heita Són ok Boðn.

translations.faulkes: They let his blood run into two vats and one cauldron. The cauldron is called Odrerir, and the vats are called Son and Bodn.
translations.brodeur: They let his blood run into two vats and a kettle. The kettle is named Óðrerir, and the vats are called Són and Boðn.
translations.literal: Let they run blood his in two vats and one kettle. Named-is kettle-the Odrerir, but vats-the named-are Son and Bodn.

original: Þeir blendu hunangi við blóðit, ok varð þar af mjöðr sá, er hverr, er af drekkr, verðr skáld eða fræðamaðr.

translations.faulkes: They mixed honey with the blood, and from it came the mead which makes anyone who drinks it a poet or a scholar.
translations.brodeur: They blended honey with the blood, and the outcome was the mead by the drinking of which any man becomes a skald or a scholar.
translations.literal: They blended honey with blood-the, and became there from mead that, which each, who from drinks, becomes skald or scholar.

original: Dvergarnir sögðu ásunum, at Kvasir hefði kafnat í mannviti, fyrir því at engi var svá fróðr, at spyrja mætti hann þess, er hann fyndi eigi við.

translations.faulkes: The dwarfs told the Aesir that Kvasir had suffocated in his own wisdom, because no one was learned enough to ask him questions he could not answer.
translations.brodeur: The dwarfs told the Aesir that Kvasir had choked with his own wisdom, because there was no man so learned that he might ask him such great store of things as he could not answer.
translations.literal: Dwarfs-the said to-Aesir, that Kvasir had choked in human-wit, because that no-one was so wise, that ask might him that, which he found not-against [answer].

original: Síðan buðu þessir dvergar heim Gillingi jötni ok konu hans.

translations.faulkes: Afterwards these dwarfs invited the giant Gilling and his wife to their home.
translations.brodeur: Then these dwarfs invited the giant Gillingr to visit them, and his wife together with him.
translations.literal: Since invited these dwarfs home Gilling giant and woman his.

original: Dvergarnir buðu Gillingi at róa á sjá með sér. En er þeir kómu út fyrir land, þá reru dvergarnir á boða ok hvolfdu skipinu.

translations.faulkes: The dwarfs invited Gilling to row out to sea with them. But when they got out from the land, the dwarfs rowed onto a reef and overturned the boat.
translations.brodeur: The dwarfs invited Gillingr to row to sea with them; but when they had gone out from the land, the dwarfs rowed upon a shoal and capsized the boat.
translations.literal: Dwarfs-the invited Gilling to row on sea with themselves. But when they came out from land, then rowed dwarfs-the on reef and overturned ship-the.

original: Gillingr var ósyndr ok týndist hann, en dvergarnir réttu skip sitt ok reru til lands.

translations.faulkes: Gilling could not swim and he perished, but the dwarfs righted their boat and rowed to land.
translations.brodeur: Gillingr was no swimmer, and he perished; but the dwarfs righted their boat and rowed to land.
translations.literal: Gilling was un-swimmable and perished he, but dwarfs-the righted ship theirs and rowed to land.

original: Þeir sögðu konu hans þessi tíðindi, en hón barst illa ok grét hátt. Þá spurði Fjalar hana, ef henni mundi léttara hugar, ef hón sæi út á sjáinn.

translations.faulkes: They told his wife this news, and she was overcome and wept loudly. Then Fjalar asked her if it would ease her mind if she looked out at the sea.
translations.brodeur: They told his wife these tidings, and she was sore distressed, and wept aloud. Then Fjalar asked her if her heart would be lighter if she might look out upon the sea.
translations.literal: They said woman his these tidings, but she bore-herself ill and wept loud. Then asked Fjalar her, if to-her would-be lighter of-mind, if she saw out on sea-the.

original: Hón játti því. Þá mælti Fjalar við Galar bróður sinn, at hann skyldi ganga upp yfir dyrnar, er hón gekk út, ok láta kvernstein falla í höfuð henni.

translations.faulkes: She agreed. Then Fjalar told his brother Galar that he should go up over the door when she went out and let a millstone fall on her head.
translations.brodeur: She said yes to it. Then Fjalar spake to Galar his brother, that he should go up over the doorway when she should go out, and let a mill-stone fall upon her head.
translations.literal: She agreed that. Then spoke Fjalar with Galar brother his, that he should go up over doors-the, when she went out, and let millstone fall in head her.

original: Hann gerði svá. En er Suttungr, sonr Gillings, spurði þetta, þá fór hann til ok tók dvergana ok flutti þá á sker nokkurt, er flæðir yfir.

translations.faulkes: He did so. When Gilling's son Suttung heard this, he went and seized the dwarfs and carried them to a certain reef which is covered at high tide.
translations.brodeur: He did so. But when Suttungr, the son of Gillingr, heard this, he fared thither and took the dwarfs and carried them out to sea, and set them on a reef which was covered at high tide.
translations.literal: He made so. But when Suttung, son of-Gilling, heard this, then went he to and took dwarfs-the and moved them on reef some, which floods over.

original: Dvergarnir báðu sér lífs ok buðu honum til yfirbóta mjöðinn dýra, ok varð þat at sætt með þeim.

translations.faulkes: The dwarfs begged for their lives and offered him the precious mead as compensation, and that became the settlement between them.
translations.brodeur: The dwarfs prayed for their lives, and offered him for atonement the precious mead; and that was the settlement between them.
translations.literal: Dwarfs-the begged for-themselves life and offered him to compensation mead-the dear, and was that at settlement with them.

original: Suttungr flutti mjöðinn heim ok hirði þar, er Hnitbjörg heita. Hann setti þar til gæzlu dóttur sína, Gunnlöðu.

translations.faulkes: Suttung carried the mead home and stored it in the place called Hnitbjorg. He put his daughter Gunnlod there to guard it.
translations.brodeur: Suttungr carried the mead home and hid it in the place called Hnitbjörg. He set his daughter Gunnlöd to keep watch over it.
translations.literal: Suttung moved mead-the home and kept [it] there, where Hnitbjorg named-is. He set there to guarding daughter his, Gunnlod.

original: Af þessu köllum vér skáldskapinn Kvasis blóð eða dverga drekku eða Suttungs mjöð.

translations.faulkes: For this reason we call poetry Kvasir's blood or dwarfs' drink or Suttung's mead.
translations.brodeur: Therefore we call poetry Kvasir's Blood, or Dwarfs' Drink... or Suttungr's Mead.
translations.literal: From this call we poetry-the Kvasir's blood or dwarfs' drink or Suttung's mead.

original: Óðinn fór heiman ok kom þar, er níu þrælar slógu gras. Hann spurði, ef þeir vildi, at hann brýndi ljá þeira.

translations.faulkes: Odin set out from home and came to where nine slaves were mowing grass. He asked if they wanted him to whet their scythes.
translations.brodeur: Odin went forth from home and came where nine thralls were mowing hay. He asked if they would have him whet their scythes.
translations.literal: Odin went from-home and came there, where nine thralls mowed grass. He asked, if they wished, that he whetted scythes theirs.

original: Hann tók hein af belti sínu ok brýndi. Þeim þótti bíta miklu betr ok vildu kaupa heinina.

translations.faulkes: He took a whetstone from his belt and whetted them. It seemed to them they cut much better and they wanted to buy the whetstone.
translations.brodeur: He took a whetstone from his belt and whetted them; it seemed to them that the scythes cut much better, and they asked to buy the whetstone.
translations.literal: He took whetstone from belt his and whetted. To-them seemed-to bite much better and wished to-buy whetstone-the.

original: Hann kastaði heininni í loft upp, en allir vildu greipa hana, ok svá fóru þeir með ljáunum, at hverr hjó á háls öðrum.

translations.faulkes: He threw the whetstone up into the air, and they all wanted to catch it, and they so handled their scythes that each cut the other's throat.
translations.brodeur: He cast the whetstone up into the air; but all wished to catch it, and they so managed that each cut the other's throat with his scythe.
translations.literal: He threw whetstone-the in air up, but all wished to-catch her, and so went they with scythes-the, that each hewed on neck other.

original: Óðinn sótti gistingu til jötuns þess, er Baugi hét, bróðir Suttungs. Hann nefndist Bölverkr.

translations.faulkes: Odin sought a night's lodging from the giant called Baugi, Suttung's brother. He called himself Bolverk.
translations.brodeur: Odin sought lodging for the night with that giant who is named Baugi, Suttungr's brother. He called himself Bölverkr.
translations.literal: Odin sought lodging to giant that, who Baugi named, brother of-Suttung. He named-himself Bolverk.

original: Bölverkr mælti við Bauga, at hann skyldi vinna níu manna verk, en hann skyldi fá honum drykk af Suttungs miði.

translations.faulkes: Bolverk told Baugi that he would do the work of nine men, provided he got him a drink of Suttung's mead.
translations.brodeur: Bölverkr told Baugi that he would do nine men's work for him, but he should ask for a drink of Suttungr's Mead as his hire.
translations.literal: Bolverk spoke with Baugi, that he should work nine men's work, but he [Baugi] should get him drink of Suttung's mead.

original: Bölverkr vann um sumarit níu manna verk. En er vetr kom, þá bað hann Bauga um leigu sína.

translations.faulkes: Bolverk did the work of nine men during the summer. When winter came, he asked Baugi for his wages.
translations.brodeur: Bölverkr performed nine men's work throughout the summer; but when winter came, he asked the giant for his hire.
translations.literal: Bolverk worked over summer-the nine men's work. But when winter came, then asked he Baugi for hire his.

original: Baugi sagði, at hann átti engan hlut í miðinum, en hann lézt fara skyldu með honum ok freista, ef þeir gæti náð honum.

translations.faulkes: Baugi said that he had no share in the mead, but said he would go with him and see if they could get hold of it.
translations.brodeur: Baugi said that he had no part in the mead, but said that he would go with him and try if they might get it.
translations.literal: Baugi said, that he had no part in mead-the, but he said-himself go should with him and try, if they might reach him [it].

original: Þeir fóru til Suttungs. Baugi sagði Suttungi kaup þeira Bölverks, en Suttungr þverneitaði.

translations.faulkes: They went to Suttung's. Baugi told Suttung of his bargain with Bolverk, but Suttung flatly refused.
translations.brodeur: They fared to Suttungr's; Baugi told Suttungr of his bargain with Bölverkr; but Suttungr flatly refused a single drop.
translations.literal: They went to Suttung's. Baugi said Suttung bargain theirs Bolverk's, but Suttung flatly-denied.

original: Þá mælti Bölverkr til Bauga, at þeir skyldu freista vélráða nokkurra. Baugi játti því.

translations.faulkes: Then Bolverk suggested to Baugi that they should try some tricks. Baugi agreed.
translations.brodeur: Then Bölverkr spake to Baugi that they must try some wiles... Baugi said that was a good plan.
translations.literal: Then spoke Bolverk to Baugi, that they should try tricks some. Baugi agreed that.

original: Bölverkr tók bor þann, er Rati heitir, ok mælti, at Baugi skyldi bora fjallið, ef borrinn biti.

translations.faulkes: Bolverk took the auger called Rati and said that Baugi should bore through the mountain if the auger bit.
translations.brodeur: Bölverkr drew forth the auger named Rati, and said that Baugi should bore through the mountain, if the auger bit.
translations.literal: Bolverk took auger that, which Rati named-is, and spoke, that Baugi should bore mountain-the, if auger-the bit.

original: Baugi boraði. Þá blés Bölverkr í borholuna, ok flugu spánirnar upp í mót honum.

translations.faulkes: Baugi bored. Then Bolverk blew into the bore-hole, and the shavings flew back at him.
translations.brodeur: Baugi bored. Then Bölverkr blew into the bore-hole, and the chips flew up in his face.
translations.literal: Baugi bored. Then blew Bolverk in bore-hole-the, and flew shavings-the up in against him.

original: Þá fann hann, at Baugi vildi véla hann. Bölverkr bað hann bora í gegnum fjallið.

translations.faulkes: Then he realized that Baugi wanted to cheat him. Bolverk told him to bore right through the mountain.
translations.brodeur: Then he knew that Baugi intended to deceive him. Bölverkr bade him bore again, through and through.
translations.literal: Then found he, that Baugi wished trick him. Bolverk asked him bore in through mountain-the.

original: Baugi boraði enn. En er Bölverkr blés í annat sinn, þá flugu spánirnar inn.

translations.faulkes: Baugi bored again. And when Bolverk blew a second time, the shavings flew inwards.
translations.brodeur: Baugi bored again; and when Bölverkr blew a second time, the chips flew inward.
translations.literal: Baugi bored yet. But when Bolverk blew in second time, then flew shavings-the in.

original: Þá brá Bölverkr sér í orms líki ok skreið í borholuna. Baugi stakk eftir honum með bornum ok missti hans.

translations.faulkes: Then Bolverk changed himself into the shape of a serpent and crawled into the bore-hole. Baugi stabbed after him with the auger and missed him.
translations.brodeur: Then Bölverkr changed himself into the likeness of a serpent and crept into the bore-hole; but Baugi stabbed after him with the auger and missed him.
translations.literal: Then cast Bolverk himself in serpent's likeness and crawled in bore-hole-the. Baugi stabbed after him with auger-the and missed him.

original: Bölverkr kom þar, er Gunnlöð var, ok lá hjá henni þrjár nætur, ok þá lofaði hón honum at drekka þrjá drykki af miðinum.

translations.faulkes: Bolverk came to where Gunnlod was and lay with her for three nights, and then she gave him permission to take three drinks of the mead.
translations.brodeur: Bölverkr fared thither where Gunnlöd was, and lay with her three nights; and then she gave him leave to drink three draughts of the mead.
translations.literal: Bolverk came there, where Gunnlod was, and lay by her three nights, and then allowed she him to drink three drinks of mead-the.

original: Í fyrsta drykk drakk hann allt ór Óðreri, en í öðrum ór Boðn, í þriðja ór Són, ok hafði hann þá allan mjöðinn.

translations.faulkes: In the first drink he drank everything in Odrerir, in the second what was in Bodn, in the third what was in Son, and then he had all the mead.
translations.brodeur: In the first draught he drank every drop out of Óðrerir; and in the second, out of Boðn; and in the third, out of Són; and then he had all the mead.
translations.literal: In first drink drank he all from Odrerir, but in second from Bodn, in third from Son, and had he then all mead-the.

original: Þá brá hann sér í arnarham ok flaug sem mest mátti hann. En er Suttungr sá flug arnarins, tók hann sinn arnarham ok flaug eftir honum.

translations.faulkes: Then he changed himself into eagle-form and flew as hard as he could. When Suttung saw the eagle's flight, he took his own eagle-form and flew after him.
translations.brodeur: Then he turned himself into the shape of an eagle and flew as furiously as he could; but when Suttungr saw the eagle's flight, he too took the shape of an eagle and flew after him.
translations.literal: Then cast he himself in eagle-hide and flew as most might he. But when Suttung saw flight eagle-the's, took he his eagle-hide and flew after him.

original: En er æsir sáu, hvar Óðinn flaug, þá settu þeir út ker sín í túnið. En er Óðinn kom inn yfir Ásgarð, þá spýtti hann miðinum í kerin.

translations.faulkes: When the Aesir saw where Odin was flying, they set their vats out in the courtyard. When Odin flew in over Asgard, he spat the mead into the vats.
translations.brodeur: When the Aesir saw where Odin flew, straightway they set their vats out in the court; and when Odin came in over Asgard, he spat up the mead into the vats.
translations.literal: But when Aesir saw, where Odin flew, then set they out vats theirs in court-the. But when Odin came in over Asgard, then spat he mead-the in vats-the.

original: En hann var svá tæpt kominn, at Suttungr var við þat at ná honum, at hann sendi aftr sumann mjöðinn, ok var þess eigi gætt. Hafði þat hverr, er vildi, ok köllum vér þat skáldfífla hlut.

translations.faulkes: He was so nearly caught that Suttung was about to seize him, and he let some of the mead go out backwards, and no one collected this. Anyone who wanted it took it, and we call this the rhymester's share.
translations.brodeur: But he was come so near to being caught that Suttungr was at his very tail, so that he sent some mead out backward, and no heed was taken of this; whosoever would might have it, and we call that the 'rhymester's share.'
translations.literal: But he was so narrowly come, that Suttung was at that to reach him, that he sent back some mead-the, and was that not guarded. Had that each, who wished, and call we that skald-fools' part.

original: En Suttungs mjöð gaf Óðinn ásunum ok þeim mönnum, er yrkja kunnu. Fyrir því köllum vér skáldskapinn feng Óðins eða drykk ásanna.

translations.faulkes: But Suttung's mead Odin gave to the Aesir and to those people who can compose. Therefore we call poetry Odin's booty or the drink of the Aesir.
translations.brodeur: But Suttungr's Mead Odin gave to the Aesir and to those men who possess the ability to compose. Therefore we call poetry Odin's Booty... or the Drink of the Aesir.
translations.literal: But Suttung's mead gave Odin to-Aesir-the and those men, who compose could. Because that call we poetry-the catch of-Odin or drink of-Aesir-the.

original: Þá mælti Ægir: 'Hvaðan af er sú list, er þér kallið skáldskap?'

translations.faulkes: Then Aegir said: 'Where does that art come from that you call poetry?'
translations.brodeur: Then spake Ægir: 'In what manner do ye say that the art of poetry is derived?'
translations.literal: Then spoke Aegir: 'From-where of is that art, which you call poetry?'

original: Bragi svarar: 'Frá því er mörg dæmi, hversu skáldskaprinn hófst, en í fyrstu var þat hráki goðanna.'

translations.faulkes: Bragi replied: 'There are many examples of how poetry began, but in the beginning it was the spittle of the gods.'
translations.brodeur: Bragi answered: 'There are many instances of the beginning of the art of poetry; but in the beginning it was the spittle of the gods.'
translations.literal: Bragi answers: 'From that are many examples, how poetry-the began, but in first was that saliva of-gods-the.'

original: Þá spyrr Ægir: 'Hvernig skal skáldskap kenna eða hversu margir eru hættir skáldskaparins?'

translations.faulkes: Then Aegir asked: 'How should poetry be described, or how many kinds of poetry are there?'
translations.brodeur: Then Ægir asked: 'In what wise is poetry to be described, and how many are the branches of poetry?'
translations.literal: Then asks Aegir: 'How shall poetry ken [describe] or how many are ways [forms] of-poetry-the?'

original: Bragi mælti: 'Tveir eru hlutir, er gera skáldskapinn: mál ok hættir.'

translations.faulkes: Bragi said: 'There are two things that make up poetry: language and meters.'
translations.brodeur: Bragi said: 'The parts which make up all poetry are two: language and meters.'
translations.literal: Bragi spoke: 'Two are things, which make poetry-the: language and meters.'

original: Hví er skáldskapr kallaðr hráki goðanna?

translations.faulkes: Why is poetry called the spittle of the gods?
translations.brodeur: Why is poetry called the spittle of the gods?
translations.literal: Why is poetry called saliva of-gods-the?

original: Því at Kvasir var skapaðr af hráka þeira, en af blóði hans var mjöðrinn gerr.

translations.faulkes: Because Kvasir was shaped from their spittle, and the mead was made from his blood.
translations.brodeur: Because Kvasir was created out of their spittle, and the mead was wrought of his blood.
translations.literal: Because that Kvasir was shaped from saliva theirs, but from blood his was mead-the made.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Óðin? Svá, at kalla hann Alföðr, Hangaguð, Farmaguð, Haptaguð.

translations.faulkes: How should Odin be referred to? By calling him All-father, God of the Hanged, God of Cargoes, God of Prisoners.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Odin be periphrased? Thus: by calling him All-father, God of the Hanged, God of Cargoes, God of Gods.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Odin? So, that call him All-father, Hanged-god, Cargo-god, Fetter-god.

original: Ok kalla hann Sigföðr, Gaut, Valföðr, Hroptatýr.

translations.faulkes: And call him Father of Victory, Gaut, Father of the Slain, Hroptatyr.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Victory-Father, Gautr, Father of the Slain, Hroptatýr.
translations.literal: And call him Victory-father, Gaut, Slain-father, Hroptatyr.

original: Hví er hann kallaðr Hangaguð eða Farmaguð?

translations.faulkes: Why is he called God of the Hanged or God of Cargoes?
translations.brodeur: Why is he called God of the Hanged and God of Cargoes?
translations.literal: Why is he called Hanged-god or Cargo-god?

original: Því at hann hékk á vindi meiði, en farmar eru kallaðir þeir, er sigla á skipum.

translations.faulkes: Because he hung on the wind-swept tree, and cargoes are what those who sail in ships are called.
translations.brodeur: Because he hanged himself on the wind-swept tree; and cargoes are called those who sail on ships.
translations.literal: Because that he hung on wind tree, but cargoes are called those, who sail on ships.

original: Hvernig skal kenna skáldskap? Svá, at kalla hann feng eða fund Óðins eða drykk hans.

translations.faulkes: How should poetry be described? By calling it Odin's booty or find or his drink.
translations.brodeur: How is poetry to be periphrased? Thus: by calling it Odin's Booty... or his find, or his drink.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] poetry? So, that call it catch or find of-Odin or drink his.

original: Ok kalla þat gjöf Óðins eða mjöð ásanna eða dverga drekku.

translations.faulkes: And call it Odin's gift or the mead of the Aesir or the drink of the dwarfs.
translations.brodeur: And calling it Odin's Gift, or the Mead of the Aesir, or the Dwarfs' Drink.
translations.literal: And call it gift of-Odin or mead of-Aesir-the or dwarfs' drink.

original: Hví er skáldskapr kallaðr Kvasis blóð?

translations.faulkes: Why is poetry called Kvasir's blood?
translations.brodeur: Why is poetry called Kvasir's Blood?
translations.literal: Why is poetry called Kvasir's blood?

original: Því at dvergarnir drápu hann ok létu blóð hans renna í kerin, ok varð af því mjöðrinn.

translations.faulkes: Because the dwarfs killed him and let his blood run into the vats, and from that the mead was made.
translations.brodeur: Because the dwarfs slew him and let his blood run into the vats, and thereof was the mead made.
translations.literal: Because that dwarfs-the slew him and let blood his run in vats-the, and became from that mead-the.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Þór? Svá, at kalla hann son Óðins ok Jarðar, föður Magna ok Móða ok Þrúðar.

translations.faulkes: How should Thor be referred to? By calling him son of Odin and Jord, father of Magni and Modi and Thrud.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Thor be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Odin and of Jörd, Father of Magni and Móði and Þrúðr.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Thor? So, that call him son of-Odin and Jord, father of-Magni and Modi and Thrud.

original: Ok kalla hann ver Sifjar, stjúpföður Ullar, banga Hrungnis eða Geirraðar.

translations.faulkes: And call him husband of Sif, stepfather of Ullr, slayer of Hrungnir or Geirrod.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Husband of Sif, Stepfather of Ullr, Slayer of Hrungnir... or of Geirröðr.
translations.literal: And call him husband of-Sif, step-father of-Ullr, slayer of-Hrungnir or Geirrod.

original: Ok kalla hann dróttin hafranna, eiganda Mjöllnis ok megingjarða.

translations.faulkes: And call him lord of the goats, owner of Mjollnir and of the belt of strength.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Lord of the Goats, Possessor of Mjöllnir and of the Girdle of Might.
translations.literal: And call him lord of-goats-the, owner of-Mjollnir and strength-belt.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Njörð? Svá, at kalla hann vana goð eða vananið, föður Freys ok Freyju.

translations.faulkes: How should Njord be referred to? By calling him a god of the Vanir or kinsman of the Vanir, father of Freyr and Freyja.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Njördr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him God of the Vanir, or Kinsman of the Vanir, Father of Freyr and Freyja.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Njord? So, that call him Vanir-god or Vanir-kin, father of-Freyr and Freyja.

original: Ok kalla hann gefanda guð ok þann, er ræðr fyrir vindum ok sjá.

translations.faulkes: And call him the giving god and the one who rules over winds and the sea.
translations.brodeur: And calling him the Giving God... and him that rules the course of the wind and the sea.
translations.literal: And call him giving god and that, who rules for winds and sea.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Frey? Svá, at kalla hann son Njarðar, bróður Freyju, Vanaguð.

translations.faulkes: How should Freyr be referred to? By calling him son of Njord, brother of Freyja, god of the Vanir.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Freyr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Njördr, Brother of Freyja, God of the Vanir.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Freyr? So, that call him son of-Njord, brother of-Freyja, Vanir-god.

original: Ok kalla hann eiganda Skíðblaðnis ok galtar þess, er Gullinbursti heitir.

translations.faulkes: And call him owner of Skidbladnir and of that boar called Gullinbursti.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Possessor of Skíðblaðnir and of the boar called Gullinbursti.
translations.literal: And call him owner of-Skidbladnir and boar that, which Gullinbursti named-is.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Heimdall? Svá, at kalla hann son níu mæðra, vörð goðanna.

translations.faulkes: How should Heimdall be referred to? By calling him son of nine mothers, watchman of the gods.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Heimdallr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Nine Mothers, Warder of the Gods.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Heimdall? So, that call him son of-nine mothers, warder of-gods-the.

original: Ok kalla hann eiganda Gulltopps ok Gjallarhorns.

translations.faulkes: And call him owner of Gulltopp and Gjallarhorn.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Possessor of Gulltoppr and of Gjallarhorn.
translations.literal: And call him owner of-Gulltopp and Gjallarhorn.

original: Hví er Heimdallr kallaðr Hallinskíði eða Gullintanni?

translations.faulkes: Why is Heimdall called Hallinskidi or Gold-tooth?
translations.brodeur: Why is Heimdallr called Hallinskíði and Golden-Toothed?
translations.literal: Why is Heimdall called Hallinskidi or Golden-tooth?

original: Hann er kallaðr Gullintanni, því at tennr hans váru af gulli. Hann er ok kallaðr Hallinskíði.

translations.faulkes: He is called Golden-toothed, because his teeth were of gold. He is also called Hallinskidi.
translations.brodeur: He is called Golden-Toothed, for his teeth were of gold. He is also called Hallinskíði.
translations.literal: He is called Golden-tooth, because that teeth his were of gold. He is also called Hallinskidi.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Tý? Svá, at kalla hann einhenda ásinn ok úlfsins fóstra, herguð, son Óðins.

translations.faulkes: How should Tyr be referred to? By calling him the one-handed Aesir and feeder of the wolf, battle-god, son of Odin.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Týr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him the One-handed God, and Feeder of the Wolf, Battle-God, Son of Odin.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Tyr? So, that call him one-handed Aesir-the and wolf's fosterer, army-god, son of-Odin.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Braga? Svá, at kalla hann Iðunnar ver, frumsmíð skáldskapar, hinn síðskeggja ás.

translations.faulkes: How should Bragi be referred to? By calling him husband of Idunn, first maker of poetry, the long-bearded Aesir.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Bragi be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Husband of Idunn, First Maker of Poetry, and the Long-bearded God.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Bragi? So, that call him Idunn's husband, first-smith of-poetry, the long-bearded Aesir.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Víðar? Svá, at kalla hann þaga ásinn, eiganda járnskósins, bana Fenrisúlfs, hefniás goðanna.

translations.faulkes: How should Vidar be referred to? By calling him the silent Aesir, owner of the iron shoe, slayer of Fenris-wolf, avenging god of the Aesir.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Víðarr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him the Silent God, Possessor of the Iron Shoe, Foe and Slayer of Fenris-Wolf, Avenging God of the Gods.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Vidar? So, that call him silent Aesir-the, owner of-iron-shoe-the, slayer of-Fenris-wolf, avenging-Aesir of-gods-the.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Vála? Svá, at kalla hann son Óðins ok Rindar, stjúpson Friggjar, hefniás Baldrs.

translations.faulkes: How should Vali be referred to? By calling him son of Odin and Rind, stepson of Frigg, avenger of Baldr.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Váli be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Odin and Rindr, Stepson of Frigg, Avenger of Baldr.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Vali? So, that call him son of-Odin and Rind, step-son of-Frigg, avenging-Aesir of-Baldr.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Höð? Svá, at kalla hann blinda ásinn, Baldrs bana, skjótanda Mistilteins, son Óðins.

translations.faulkes: How should Hod be referred to? By calling him the blind god, Baldr's slayer, shooter of the mistletoe, son of Odin.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Höðr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him the Blind God, Baldr's Slayer, Thrower of the Mistletoe, Son of Odin.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Hod? So, that call him blind Aesir-the, Baldr's slayer, shooter of-Mistletoe, son of-Odin.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Ull? Svá, at kalla hann son Sifjar, stjúpsön Þórs, skíðás, bogás, veiðiás.

translations.faulkes: How should Ullr be referred to? By calling him son of Sif, stepson of Thor, ski-god, bow-god, hunting-god.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Ullr be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Sif, Stepson of Thor, Ski-God, Bow-God, Hunting-God.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Ull? So, that call him son of-Sif, step-son of-Thor, ski-Aesir, bow-Aesir, hunting-Aesir.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Forseta? Svá, at kalla hann son Baldrs ok Nönnu, þann er sættir goð ok menn.

translations.faulkes: How should Forseti be referred to? By calling him son of Baldr and Nanna, the one who reconciles gods and men.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Forseti be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Baldr and Nanna, and him that settles all disputes of gods and men.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Forseti? So, that call him son of-Baldr and Nanna, that-one who reconciles gods and men.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Loka? Svá, at kalla hann son Farbauta ok Laufeyjar eða Nal, bróður Býleists ok Helblinda.

translations.faulkes: How should Loki be referred to? By calling him son of Farbauti and Laufey or Nal, brother of Byleist and Helblindi.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Loki be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Fárbauti and Laufey, or of Nál, Brother of Býleistr and of Helblindi.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Loki? So, that call him son of-Farbauti and Laufey or Nal, brother of-Byleist and Helblindi.

original: Ok kalla hann föður Fenrisúlfs ok Miðgarðsorms ok Heljar, rægispæi goðanna, vélanda þeira.

translations.faulkes: And call him father of Fenris-wolf and Midgard-serpent and Hel, slanderer of the gods, deceiver of them.
translations.brodeur: And calling him Father of the Fenris-Wolf and of the Midgard-Serpent and of Hel... Slanderer of the Gods, Deceiver of them.
translations.literal: And call him father of-Fenris-wolf and Midgard-serpent and Hel, slander-maker of-gods-the, deceiver of-them.

original: Hví er hann kallaðr þjófr Brísingamens? Því at hann ok Heimdallr barðist um þat at Singasteini í sela líkjum.

translations.faulkes: Why is he called thief of Brisingamen? Because he and Heimdall fought for it at Singastein in the form of seals.
translations.brodeur: Why is he called Thief of Brísingamen? Because he and Heimdallr contended for it at Singasteinn in the form of seals.
translations.literal: Why is he called thief of-Brisingamen? Because that he and Heimdall fought for that at Singastein in seals' likenesses.

original: Loki er ok kallaðr Lopt eða Hveðrungr.

translations.faulkes: Loki is also called Lopt or Hvedrung.
translations.brodeur: Loki is also called Loptr, or Hveðrungr.
translations.literal: Loki is also called Lopt or Hvedrung.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Frigg? Svá, at kalla hana dóttur Fjörgyns, konu Óðins, móður Baldrs.

translations.faulkes: How should Frigg be referred to? By calling her daughter of Fjorgyn, wife of Odin, mother of Baldr.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Frigg be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Daughter of Fjörgynn, Wife of Odin, Mother of Baldr.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Frigg? So, that call her daughter of-Fjorgyn, wife of-Odin, mother of-Baldr.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Freyju? Svá, at kalla hana dóttur Njarðar, systur Freys, konu Óðs, móður Hnossar.

translations.faulkes: How should Freyja be referred to? By calling her daughter of Njord, sister of Freyr, wife of Od, mother of Hnoss.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Freyja be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Daughter of Njördr, Sister of Freyr, Wife of Óðr, Mother of Hnoss.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Freyja? So, that call her daughter of-Njord, sister of-Freyr, wife of-Od, mother of-Hnoss.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Sif? Svá, at kalla hana konu Þórs, móður Ullar, hina hárfögru goðmög.

translations.faulkes: How should Sif be referred to? By calling her wife of Thor, mother of Ullr, the fair-haired deity.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Sif be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Wife of Thor, Mother of Ullr, and the Fair-haired Goddess.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Sif? So, that call her wife of-Thor, mother of-Ullr, the fair-haired goddess.

original: Hvernig skal kenna Iðunni? Svá, at kalla hana konu Braga, gæzlukonu eplanna.

translations.faulkes: How should Idunn be referred to? By calling her wife of Bragi, keeper of the apples.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should Idunn be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Wife of Bragi, and Keeper of the Apples.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] Idunn? So, that call her wife of-Bragi, keeper-woman of-apples-the.

original: Hví er gull kallað haddr Sifjar?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called Sif's hair?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called Sif's Hair?
translations.literal: Why is gold called hair of-Sif?

original: Loki Laufeyjarson hafði þat gert til lævísi at klippa hár allt af Sif. En er Þórr varð þess varr, tók hann Loka ok mundi lemja hvert bein í honum.

translations.faulkes: Loki Laufeyjarson had done it for mischief to cut all the hair off Sif. When Thor found out, he seized Loki and would have crushed every bone in him.
translations.brodeur: Loki Laufeyjarson, for mischief's sake, cut off all Sif's hair. But when Thor learned of this, he seized Loki and would have broken every bone in him.
translations.literal: Loki Laufeyjarson had that made to mischief to clip hair all from Sif. But when Thor became of-that aware, took he Loki and would crush every bone in him.

original: Loki hét því, at hann skyldi fá af svartálfum, at þeir skyldu gera af gulli hadd þann, er svá skyldi vaxa sem hár annað.

translations.faulkes: Loki promised that he would get the black-elves to make a head of hair out of gold that would grow like other hair.
translations.brodeur: Loki swore that he would get the Black Elves to make Sif a head of hair of gold, such that it should grow as other hair.
translations.literal: Loki promised that, that he should get from black-elves, that they should make from gold hair that, which so should grow as hair other.

original: Síðan fór Loki til dverga þeira, er heita Ívaldasynir, ok gerðu þeir haddinn ok Skíðblaðni ok spjót þat, er Óðinn á, er Gungnir heitir.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki went to the dwarfs called Ivaldi's sons, and they made the hair and Skidbladnir and the spear that Odin has, which is called Gungnir.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki fared to those dwarfs who are called Ivaldi's Sons; and they wrought the hair, and Skíðblaðnir, and the spear which Odin has, called Gungnir.
translations.literal: Since went Loki to dwarfs those, who named-are Ivaldi's-sons, and made they hair-the and Skidbladnir and spear that, which Odin has, which Gungnir named-is.

original: Þá veðjaði Loki höfði sínu við dverg þann, er Brokkr heitir, hvárt bróðir hans, Eitri, mundi gera jafngóða gripi þrjá sem þessir váru.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki wagered his head with the dwarf called Brokk that his brother Eitri would not be able to make three other precious things as good as these were.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki wagered his head with the dwarf named Brokkr that Brokkr's brother, Eitri, should not make three other precious things as good as these were.
translations.literal: Then wagered Loki head his with dwarf that, who Brokk named-is, whether brother his, Eitri, would make equally-good items three as these were.

original: Eitri lagði svínshúð í aflinn ok bað Brokk blása ok létta eigi fyrr en hann tæki þat ór aflinum.

translations.faulkes: Eitri put a pig-skin in the forge and told Brokk to blow and not stop until he took it out of the forge.
translations.brodeur: Eitri laid a swine-skin in the hearth and bade Brokkr blow, and did not let up until he himself should take out what he had laid therein.
translations.literal: Eitri laid swine's-hide in forge-the and asked Brokk blow and stop not earlier than he took that from forge-the.

original: En er hann gekk ór smiðjunni, en hinn blés, þá settist fluga ein á hönd honum ok stakk.

translations.faulkes: But when he went out of the smithy and the other was blowing, a fly settled on his hand and stung him.
translations.brodeur: But as soon as Eitri went out of the smithy, while Brokkr was blowing, a fly settled on Brokkr's hand and stung him.
translations.literal: But when he went out-of smithy-the, but the-other blew, then sat-itself fly one on hand him and stung.

original: En hann blés sem áðr, þar til er Eitri tók ór aflinum, ok var þat göltr ok váru burstirnar af gulli.

translations.faulkes: But he blew as before, until Eitri took it out of the forge, and it was a boar and its bristles were of gold.
translations.brodeur: Yet he blew on as before, until Eitri took the work out of the hearth; and it was a boar, with mane and bristles of gold.
translations.literal: But he blew as before, there until Eitri took from forge-the, and was that boar and were bristles-the of gold.

original: Því næst lagði hann gull í aflinn ok bað hann blása ok létta eigi blæstrinum.

translations.faulkes: Next he put gold in the forge and told him to blow and not stop the blowing.
translations.brodeur: Then Eitri laid gold in the hearth and bade Brokkr blow and not cease from his blast.
translations.literal: That next laid he gold in forge-the and asked him blow and stop not blowing-the.

original: Flugan kom aftr ok settist á háls honum ok stakk nú hálfu fastara. En hann blés þar til er Eitri tók ór aflinum gullhring þann, er Draupnir heitir.

translations.faulkes: The fly came back and settled on his neck and now stung twice as hard. But he blew until Eitri took out of the forge the gold ring called Draupnir.
translations.brodeur: The fly came again and settled on his neck, and stung him now twice as hard; but he blew on until Eitri took from the hearth the gold ring which is called Draupnir.
translations.literal: Fly-the came back and sat-itself on neck him and stung now half [twice] faster. But he blew there until Eitri took from forge-the gold-ring that, which Draupnir named-is.

original: Þá lagði hann járn í aflinn ok bað hann blása, ok sagði, at ónýtt mundi verða, ef blæstrinn félli.

translations.faulkes: Then he put iron in the forge and told him to blow, and said it would be spoiled if the blowing failed.
translations.brodeur: Then Eitri laid iron in the hearth and bade him blow, saying that it would be spoiled if the blast failed.
translations.literal: Then laid he iron in forge-the and asked him blow, and said, that useless would become, if blowing-the fell [ceased].

original: Þá settist flugan milli augna honum ok stakk hvarmana, svá at blóðið rann í augun, at hann sá ekki.

translations.faulkes: Then the fly settled between his eyes and stung the eyelids, so that the blood ran into his eyes so that he could not see.
translations.brodeur: Then the fly settled between his eyes and stung his eyelid, so that the blood ran down into his eyes and he could not see.
translations.literal: Then sat-itself fly-the between eyes him and stung eyelids-the, so that blood-the ran in eyes-the, that he saw not.

original: Þá greip hann til hendinni sem skjótast, meðan borrinn fór, ok sveipaði af sér flugunni. En þá tók Eitri ór aflinum hamarinn.

translations.faulkes: Then he reached out his hand as quickly as possible, while the bellows went down, and swept the fly away. Then Eitri took the hammer out of the forge.
translations.brodeur: Then he clutched at it with his hand as swiftly as he could—while the bellows grew flat—and swept the fly from him. Then Eitri took the hammer from the hearth.
translations.literal: Then gripped he to hand-the as quickest, while bellows-the went, and swept from himself fly-the. But then took Eitri from forge-the hammer-the.

original: Hamarinn var mjög góðr, en þat var áfátt, at skaftið var heldr skammt.

translations.faulkes: The hammer was very good, but there was this flaw, that the handle was rather short.
translations.brodeur: The hammer was very good, but this was the flaw: that the handle was somewhat short.
translations.literal: Hammer-the was much good, but that was lacking, that handle-the was rather short.

original: Dvergarnir gáfu Óðni spjótið Gungni, en Þór haddinn, en Frey Skíðblaðni.

translations.faulkes: The dwarfs gave Odin the spear Gungnir, and Thor the hair, and Freyr Skidbladnir.
translations.brodeur: The dwarfs gave Odin the spear Gungnir; to Thor, the hair; and to Freyr, Skíðblaðnir.
translations.literal: Dwarfs-the gave Odin spear-the Gungnir, but Thor hair-the, but Freyr Skidbladnir.

original: En Brokkr bar fram sína gripi ok gaf Óðni hringinn, en Frey göltinn, en Þór hamarinn.

translations.faulkes: And Brokk brought forward his treasures and gave Odin the ring, and Freyr the boar, and Thor the hammer.
translations.brodeur: Then Brokkr brought forward his precious things: he gave Odin the ring; to Freyr, the boar; and to Thor, the hammer.
translations.literal: But Brokk bore forward his treasures and gave Odin ring-the, but Freyr boar-the, but Thor hammer-the.

original: Spjótið nam aldri staðar í lagi. Haddrinn var af gulli ok greri sem hár. Skíðblaðnir hafði byr, þegar er segl var uppi, en mátti vefja saman sem dúk.

translations.faulkes: The spear never stopped in its thrust. The hair was of gold and grew like real hair. Skidbladnir had a fair wind as soon as its sail was up, but could be folded together like a cloth.
translations.brodeur: The spear should never stop in its thrust; the hair, as soon as it came on Sif's head, should grow fast to the flesh; and Skíðblaðnir should have a fair wind... but might be folded together like a napkin.
translations.literal: Spear-the took never stead [stopped] in thrust. Hair-the was of gold and grew as hair. Skidbladnir had fair-wind, when that sail was up, but might weave [fold] together as cloth.

original: Hringrinn Draupnir draup níunda hverja nótt átta hringum jafnhöfnum.

translations.faulkes: The ring Draupnir dripped eight rings of equal weight every ninth night.
translations.brodeur: From the ring Draupnir, every ninth night, there should drop eight rings of equal weight.
translations.literal: Ring-the Draupnir dripped ninth each night eight rings equally-heavy.

original: Göltinn mátti renna loft ok lög nótt ok dag meir en hverr hestr, ok varð aldri svá myrkt, at eigi væri ljóst af burstunum.

translations.faulkes: The boar could run through air and sea night and day faster than any horse, and it never became so dark that there was not light from its bristles.
translations.brodeur: To Freyr he gave the boar, saying that it could run through air and water by night and by day, better than any horse... never should it be so dark that there should not be sufficient light where he went.
translations.literal: Boar-the might run air and sea night and day more than each horse, and became never so dark, that not was light from bristles-the.

original: En Þór gaf hann hamarinn ok mælti, at hann mætti ljósta svá mikit sem hann vildi, ok mundi eigi hamarinn bila.

translations.faulkes: And to Thor he gave the hammer and said that he might strike as hard as he liked, and the hammer would not fail.
translations.brodeur: Then he gave the hammer to Thor, and said that he might smite as hard as he would, and the hammer should not fail.
translations.literal: But Thor gave he hammer-the and spoke, that he might strike so much as he wished, and would not hammer-the fail.

original: Dæmdu æsirnir, at hamarinn væri beztr af öllum gripunum ok mesta vörn í fyrir hrímþursum.

translations.faulkes: The Aesir judged that the hammer was the best of all the treasures and the greatest protection against frost-giants.
translations.brodeur: The Aesir gave judgment that the hammer was best of all the precious works, and in it was the greatest defense against the Rime-Giants.
translations.literal: Judged Aesir-the, that hammer-the was best of all treasures-the and greatest defense in against rime-giants.

original: Þá vildi Brokkr hafa höfuð Loka. En Loki bauð at leysa höfuð sitt, en dvergrinn sagði, at þess var engi ván.

translations.faulkes: Then Brokk wanted Loki's head. But Loki offered to ransom his head, but the dwarf said there was no hope of that.
translations.brodeur: Then Brokkr claimed his wager, Loki's head. Loki offered to redeem it, but the dwarf said that there was no hope of this.
translations.literal: Then wished Brokk have head Loki's. But Loki offered to loose head his, but dwarf-the said, that of-that was no hope.

original: Loki mælti: 'Taktu mig þá.' En er hann vildi taka hann, þá var hann fjarri. Loki átti skó, er hann rann á loft ok lög.

translations.faulkes: Loki said: 'Take me then.' But when he wanted to take him, he was far away. Loki had shoes with which he ran through air and sea.
translations.brodeur: Loki said: 'Take me, then.' But when he would have taken him, Loki was far away; for he had shoes with which he ran through air and over water.
translations.literal: Loki spoke: 'Take me then.' But when he wished take him, then was he far. Loki had shoes, that he ran on air and sea.

original: Þá bað dvergrinn Þór at taka hann, en hann gerði svá. Þá vildi dvergrinn höggva af honum höfuðið. En Loki sagði, at hann átti höfuðit, en eigi hálsinn.

translations.faulkes: Then the dwarf asked Thor to take him, and he did so. Then the dwarf wanted to cut off his head. But Loki said that he owned the head, but not the neck.
translations.brodeur: Then the dwarf prayed Thor to catch him, and Thor did so. Then the dwarf would have stricken off his head; but Loki said that he might have the head, but not the neck.
translations.literal: Then asked dwarf-the Thor to take him, and he made so. Then wished dwarf-the hew from him head-the. But Loki said, that he had head-the, but not neck-the.

original: Þá tók dvergrinn þveng ok kníf ok vildi stinga raufar á vörum Loka ok syfja saman munninn, en knífrinn beit ekki. Þá mælti hann, at betri væri alr föður hans. En jafnskjótt var þar alrinn, ok beit hann varirnar.

translations.faulkes: Then the dwarf took a thong and a knife and wanted to pierce holes in Loki's lips and sew the mouth together, but the knife would not cut. Then he said his father's awl would be better. And immediately the awl was there, and it pierced the lips.
translations.brodeur: Then the dwarf took a thong and a knife, and would have bored holes in Loki's lips and sewn his mouth together, but the knife did not bite. Then he said that his father's awl would be better; and straightway the awl was there, and it pierced the lips.
translations.literal: Then took dwarf-the thong and knife and wished pierce holes on lips Loki's and sew together mouth-the, but knife-the bit not. Then spoke he, that better would-be awl father's his. And equally-soon was there awl-the, and bit he lips-the.

original: Hann syfði saman varirnar, ok raust hann ór raufunum. Þvengrinn heitir Vartari.

translations.faulkes: He sewed the lips together, and he ripped them out through the holes. The thong is called Vartari.
translations.brodeur: He sewed the lips together, and Loki wriggled them out of the holes. The thong with which the mouth was sewn together is called Vartari.
translations.literal: He sewed together lips-the, and ripped he from holes-the. Thong-the named-is Vartari.

original: Hví er gull kallað eldr Ægis?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called Aegir's fire?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called Aegir's Fire?
translations.literal: Why is gold called fire of-Aegir?

original: Saga er til þess, at Ægir fór til Ásgarðs til heimboðs. En er hann var búinn til heimferðar, þá bauð hann Óðni ok öllum ásum til sín at þrem mánuðum.

translations.faulkes: The story is that Aegir went to Asgard for a visit. When he was ready for the journey home, he invited Odin and all the Aesir to visit him in three months' time.
translations.brodeur: This is the tale thereof: Ægir went to Asgard to a banquet; but when he was ready for the journey home, he invited Odin and all the Aesir to visit him three months thence.
translations.literal: Story is to that, that Aegir went to Asgard to home-invitation. But when he was ready to home-journey, then invited he Odin and all Aesir to himself at three months.

original: Fyrst fór Óðinn ok Njörðr, Freyr, Týr, Bragi, Víðarr, Loki. Svá ok ásynjur: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Skadi, Iðunn, Sif.

translations.faulkes: First went Odin and Njord, Freyr, Tyr, Bragi, Vidar, Loki. Also the goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Skadi, Idunn, Sif.
translations.brodeur: First went Odin and Njördr, Freyr, Týr, Bragi, Víðarr, Loki; likewise the Goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Skaði, Idunn, Sif.
translations.literal: First went Odin and Njord, Freyr, Tyr, Bragi, Vidar, Loki. So also goddesses: Frigg, Freyja, Gefjun, Skadi, Idunn, Sif.

original: Þórr var eigi þar. Hann var farinn í austrveg at drepa tröll. En er goðin váru sezt í sæti, þá lét Ægir bera inn á hallargólfit lýsigull, ok lýsti þat höllina.

translations.faulkes: Thor was not there. He had gone into the eastern regions to kill trolls. But when the gods had sat in their seats, Aegir had bright gold brought onto the hall floor, and it lit up the hall.
translations.brodeur: Thor was not there, for he had gone into the Eastern Free-lands to slay trolls. But when the gods had sat down in their places, straightway Ægir had bright gold brought in upon the floor of the hall, and the gold gave out light.
translations.literal: Thor was not there. He was gone in east-way to slay trolls. But when gods-the were sat in seats, then let Aegir bear in on hall-floor-the bright-gold, and lit that hall-the.

original: Var þat haft þar fyrir ljós sem í Valhöll váru sverðin. Þá bar Loki saman orð við goðin öll.

translations.faulkes: It was used there as light just as swords were in Valhall. Then Loki exchanged words with all the gods.
translations.brodeur: It was used there for light, even as swords are used in Valhall. Then Loki bandied words with all the gods.
translations.literal: Was that had there for light as in Valhall were swords-the. Then bore Loki together words with gods-the all.

original: Fyrir því er gull kallað eldr eða ljós eða glóð Ægis eða dætra hans. Ok af þessum heitum er nú gull kallað sævar eldr ok öllum heitum sjávar.

translations.faulkes: For this reason gold is called fire or light or brightness of Aegir or of his daughters. And because of these names, gold is now called fire of the sea and by all names for the sea.
translations.brodeur: Therefore gold is called Fire, or Light, or Brightness of Ægir, or of his daughters; and from these periphrases gold is now called Fire of the Sea, and by all names of the sea.
translations.literal: Because that is gold called fire or light or glow of-Aegir or daughters his. And from these names is now gold called sea's fire and all names of-sea.

original: Hví er gull kallað gler dætra Ægis?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the glass of Aegir's daughters?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Glass of Aegir's Daughters?
translations.literal: Why is gold called glass of-daughters of-Aegir?

original: Því at þær eru dætur hans, en glerit er gegnsætt sem sjárinn. Ok því er gull kallað barr eða lauf eða skógr sjávar.

translations.faulkes: Because they are his daughters, and glass is transparent like the sea. And therefore gold is called needles or foliage or wood of the sea.
translations.brodeur: Because they are his daughters, and glass is transparent like the sea; and for this reason gold is called needles, or foliage, or wood of the sea.
translations.literal: Because that they are daughters his, but glass-the is transparent as sea-the. And therefore is gold called needles or leaf or forest of-sea.

original: Hví er gull kallað lög eða dreyri eða sviti dverga?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the liquid or blood or sweat of dwarfs?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Liquid, or the Blood, or the Sweat of Dwarfs?
translations.literal: Why is gold called liquid or blood or sweat of-dwarfs?

original: Því at þeir sköpuðu þat, er þeir gerðu hringinn Draupni eða aðra gripi.

translations.faulkes: Because they created it when they made the ring Draupnir or other treasures.
translations.brodeur: Because they created it when they wrought the ring Draupnir and other precious works.
translations.literal: Because that they shaped that, when they made ring-the Draupnir or other treasures.

original: Hví er gull kallað fleyritar dverganna?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the float-letters of the dwarfs?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Float-Letters of the Dwarfs?
translations.literal: Why is gold called float-letters of-dwarfs-the?

original: Því at þeir leyna því í rúnum eða í klettum, ok er þat kallað mál eða ræða þeira.

translations.faulkes: Because they hide it in runes or in rocks, and it is called their speech or conversation.
translations.brodeur: Because they hide it in runes or in the cliffs, and it is called their speech or their discourse.
translations.literal: Because that they hide that in runes or in cliffs, and is that called speech or talk theirs.

original: Hví er gull kallað korn eða sáð fylli Fýrisvalla?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the grain or seed of the fullness of Fyris-plain?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Grain, or the Seed, of the Abundance of the Fyris-Plain?
translations.literal: Why is gold called grain or seed of-fullness of-Fyrisvalla?

original: Saga er til þess, at Hrólfr kraki konungr fór til Uppsala at sækja föðurarf sinn hjá Aðilsi konungi.
