translations.faulkes: The story is that King Hrolf Kraki went to Uppsala to claim his father's inheritance from King Adils.
translations.brodeur: This is the tale thereof: King Hrólfr Kraki went to Uppsala to claim his father's heritage from King Aðils.
translations.literal: Story is to that, that Hrolf kraki king went to Uppsala to seek father-inheritance his with Adils king.

original: En er þeir flýðu yfir Fýrisvöllu, þá kastaði Hrólfr kraki gullinu ór dýrshorni einu miklu.

translations.faulkes: When they were fleeing across Fyris-plain, Hrolf Kraki threw the gold out of a large horn of a beast.
translations.brodeur: But as they were fleeing over the Fyris-Plain, King Hrólfr Kraki cast the gold out of a great beast-horn.
translations.literal: But when they fled over Fyrisvalla, then threw Hrolf kraki gold-the from beast-horn one great.

original: Svíar rann eftir gullinu ok tóku þat upp, en Hrólfr ok hans menn komust undan. Ok því er gullit kallað sáð Hrólfs kraka.

translations.faulkes: The Swedes ran after the gold and picked it up, and Hrolf and his men escaped. And therefore gold is called the seed of Hrolf Kraki.
translations.brodeur: The Swedes ran after the gold and gathered it up, and Hrólfr and his men escaped; wherefore gold is called the Seed of Hrólfr Kraki.
translations.literal: Swedes ran after gold-the and took that up, but Hrolf and his men came away. And therefore is gold-the called seed of-Hrolf kraki.

original: Hví er gull kallað rógmálm eða sakarmerki?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the metal of strife or the token of dispute?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Metal of Strife, or the Mark of Dispute?
translations.literal: Why is gold called strife-metal or cause-mark?

original: Því at þat veldr jafnan deilum ok vígum með mönnum. Ok því er þat kallað eldr handa eða liða.

translations.faulkes: Because it constantly causes quarrels and killings among men. And therefore it is called the fire of hands or of joints.
translations.brodeur: Because it is ever the cause of quarrels and of slayings among men; wherefore it is called the Fire of the Hands, or of the Joints.
translations.literal: Because that it causes always disputes and slayings among men. And therefore is that called fire of-hands or of-joints.

original: Hví er gull kallað Oturgjöld?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called Otter's Ransom?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called Otter-Guerdon?
translations.literal: Why is gold called Otter-payment?

original: Saga er til þess, at þeir fóru þrír æsir saman, Óðinn ok Hænir ok Loki, ok kómu til Forsar nokkurs.

translations.faulkes: The story is that three Aesir set out together, Odin and Hoenir and Loki, and came to a certain waterfall.
translations.brodeur: This is the tale thereof: Three of the Aesir went forth to explore the whole world, Odin and Hönir and Loki; and they came to a certain stream.
translations.literal: Story is to that, that they went three Aesir together, Odin and Hoenir and Loki, and came to waterfall some.

original: Þar sat otr einn við forsinn ok hafði tekit lax ór forsinum ok át blundandi.

translations.faulkes: An otter was sitting by the waterfall and had caught a salmon from the fall and was eating it with its eyes shut.
translations.brodeur: An otter sat by the stream, and had taken a salmon out of the water, and was eating it, half-blindly.
translations.literal: There sat otter one by waterfall-the and had taken salmon from waterfall-the and ate dozing.

original: Þá tók Loki stein ok kastaði at otrinum ok laust í höfuð honum. Þá hrósaði Loki veiði sinni.

translations.faulkes: Then Loki took a stone and threw it at the otter and hit it in the head. Then Loki boasted of his catch.
translations.brodeur: Then Loki took up a stone and cast it at the otter, and struck its head. Then Loki boasted of his catch.
translations.literal: Then took Loki stone and threw at otter-the and struck in head him. Then boasted Loki catch his.

original: Þeir fóru þá með otrinn ok kómu til bæjar eins. Sá bóndi hét Hreiðmarr. Hann var mjög fjölkunnigr.

translations.faulkes: They then went on with the otter and came to a certain farm. The farmer was called Hreidmar. He was very skilled in magic.
translations.brodeur: They took the otter with them and came to a certain farm, where they sought lodging. The husbandman who dwelt there was named Hreiðmarr; he was a man of much substance, and very skilled in magic.
translations.literal: They went then with otter-the and came to farm one. That farmer named-was Hreidmar. He was much magic-skilled.

original: Þeir báðust gistingar ok sögðu, at þeir höfðu veiði mikla, ok sýndu honum otrinn.

translations.faulkes: They asked for a night's lodging and said they had a large catch, and showed him the otter.
translations.brodeur: They asked for a night's lodging and said that they were well-provided with food, and showed him their catch.
translations.literal: They asked-for lodging and said, that they had catch great, and showed him otter-the.

original: En er Hreiðmarr sá otrinn, þá kallaði hann sonu sína, Fáfni ok Regin, ok sagði, at Otr bróðir þeira væri drepinn.

translations.faulkes: But when Hreidmar saw the otter, he called his sons, Fafnir and Regin, and said that their brother Otr had been killed.
translations.brodeur: But when Hreiðmarr saw the otter, he called his sons, Fáfnir and Reginn, and said that Otr their brother was slain.
translations.literal: But when Hreidmar saw otter-the, then called he sons his, Fafnir and Regin, and said, that Otr brother theirs was slain.

original: Þá gengu þeir feðgar at ásunum ok tóku þá höndum ok fjötruðu þá, ok sögðu, at otrinn var sonr Hreiðmars.

translations.faulkes: Then the father and sons went for the Aesir and seized them and bound them, and said the otter was Hreidmar's son.
translations.brodeur: Then the father and sons went at the Aesir, and took them captive and bound them; and told them then that the otter was Hreiðmarr's son.
translations.literal: Then went they father-and-sons at Aesir-the and took them hands [by hands] and fettered them, and said, that otter-the was son of-Hreidmar.

original: Æsirnir buðu fyrir sig fjörlausn, svá mikit fé sem Hreiðmarr vildi sjálfr dæma. Var þat at sætt gert með þeim.

translations.faulkes: The Aesir offered as ransom for their lives as much money as Hreidmar himself cared to demand. A settlement was made to that effect between them.
translations.brodeur: The Aesir offered as ransom for their lives as much wealth as Hreiðmarr himself should care to determine; and this was established as a covenant between them, and confirmed with oaths.
translations.literal: Aesir-the offered for themselves life-ransom, so much wealth as Hreidmar wished himself judge. Was that at settlement made with them.

original: Hreiðmarr tók otrsbeltið ok mælti, at þeir skyldu fylla belginn með rauðu gulli ok hylja hann allan utan.

translations.faulkes: Hreidmar took the otter-skin and said that they must fill the skin with red gold and cover it all on the outside.
translations.brodeur: Hreiðmarr took the otter-skin and said that they must fill the skin with red gold, and thereafter also cover it all on the outside with red gold.
translations.literal: Hreidmar took otter-skin-the and spoke, that they should fill skin-the with red gold and hide [cover] it all outside.

original: Óðinn sendi Loka í Svartálfaheima, ok kom hann til dvergs þess, er Andvari heitir.

translations.faulkes: Odin sent Loki to the world of black-elves, and he came to a certain dwarf called Andvari.
translations.brodeur: Odin sent Loki into the Land of the Black Elves, and he came to a certain dwarf who is called Andvari.
translations.literal: Odin sent Loki in Black-elf-homes, and came he to dwarf that, who Andvari named-is.

original: Hann var í vatni í geddu líki. Loki tók hann ok krafði hann gulls alls, er hann átti í steini sínum.

translations.faulkes: He was in a lake in the shape of a pike. Loki caught him and demanded from him all the gold he had in his rock.
translations.brodeur: He was in the water in the likeness of a pike; and Loki took him captive and required of him as ransom all the gold which he had in his rock.
translations.literal: He was in water in pike's likeness. Loki took him and demanded him gold all, which he had in rock his.

original: En er dvergrinn bar fram gullit, þá var þat mjög mikit fé. En dvergrinn tók undir hönd sér einn lítinn gullhring.

translations.faulkes: And when the dwarf brought out the gold, it was a very great amount of wealth. But the dwarf tucked under his arm one small gold ring.
translations.brodeur: And when the dwarf brought forth the gold, it was a right great wealth; but the dwarf hid under his arm one small gold ring.
translations.literal: But when dwarf-the bore forward gold-the, then was that much great wealth. But dwarf-the took under hand his one little gold-ring.

original: Loki sá þat ok bað hann fá sér hringinn. Dvergrinn bað hann taka eigi hringinn frá sér, ok sagði, at hann mátti ávaxta af honum fé, ef hann ætti hringinn.

translations.faulkes: Loki saw it and told him to give him the ring. The dwarf begged him not to take the ring from him, and said that he could multiply his wealth from it if he kept the ring.
translations.brodeur: Loki saw it and bade him give up the ring. The dwarf prayed him not to take the ring from him, saying that from this ring he could multiply wealth for himself if he kept it.
translations.literal: Loki saw that and asked him get himself ring-the. Dwarf-the asked him take not ring-the from himself, and said, that he might multiply from it wealth, if he had ring-the.

original: Loki sagði, at hann skyldi ekki hafa einn pening eftir, ok tók hringinn af honum.

translations.faulkes: Loki said that he should not have a single penny left, and took the ring from him.
translations.brodeur: Loki said that he should not have one penny left, and took the ring from him.
translations.literal: Loki said, that he should not have one penny after, and took ring-the from him.

original: Þá gekk dvergrinn í steininn ok mælti, at sá hringr skyldi vera hverjum manni at bana, er hann ætti.

translations.faulkes: Then the dwarf went into the rock and said that that ring should be the death of whoever owned it.
translations.brodeur: Then the dwarf went into the hollow of the rock and said that that ring should be the undoing of every man who should possess it.
translations.literal: Then went dwarf-the in rock-the and spoke, that that ring should be each man to death, who it had.

original: Loki fór til Hreiðmars ok sýndi Óðni gullit. En er hann sá hringinn, þá sýndist honum fagr ok tók hann ór fénu, en greiddi Hreiðmari gullit.

translations.faulkes: Loki went to Hreidmar's and showed Odin the gold. But when he saw the ring, it seemed beautiful to him and he took it out of the treasure and paid Hreidmar the gold.
translations.brodeur: Loki fared to Hreiðmarr and showed Odin the gold; but when Odin saw the ring, it seemed fair to him, and he took it away from the treasure, but paid the rest of the gold to Hreiðmarr.
translations.literal: Loki went to Hreidmar's and showed Odin gold-the. But when he saw ring-the, then seemed-to him fair and took he [it] from wealth-the, but paid Hreidmar gold-the.

original: Hreiðmarr fyllti otrsbeltið ok reisti upp á fætr, ok varð þá at hylja með gullinu.

translations.faulkes: Hreidmar filled the otter-skin and stood it on its feet, and it then had to be covered with the gold.
translations.brodeur: Hreiðmarr filled the otter-skin as full as he could, and set it on its feet. Then the gods went to it to cover the skin with gold.
translations.literal: Hreidmar filled otter-skin-the and raised up on feet, and was then [needed] to hide with gold-the.

original: En er þat var gert, þá gekk Hreiðmarr til ok sá, at eitt granahár stóð út, ok bað hylja þat.

translations.faulkes: And when that was done, Hreidmar went up and saw that one whisker was sticking out, and told them to cover it.
translations.brodeur: But when that was done, Hreiðmarr went to it and looked, and saw one whisker-hair, and bade them cover it.
translations.literal: But when that was made, then went Hreidmar to and saw, that one whisker-hair stood out, and asked hide that.

original: Þá dró Óðinn fram hringinn Andvaranaut ok huldi hárið. Þá mælti Loki, at nú væri goldit otrgjöldin.

translations.faulkes: Then Odin drew out the ring Andvaranaut and covered the hair. Then Loki said that the otter's ransom had now been paid.
translations.brodeur: Then Odin drew forth the ring Andvaranaut, and covered the hair, and said that they were now free of their ransom.
translations.literal: Then drew Odin forward ring-the Andvaranaut and hid hair-the. Then spoke Loki, that now was paid otter-payment-the.

original: En er Loki var lauss orðinn, þá mælti hann, at þat gull skyldi verða þeim at bana, er ætti.

translations.faulkes: When Loki had been released, he said that the gold should be the death of whoever owned it.
translations.brodeur: But when Loki was loosed, he spake and said that that gold should be the death of him who should possess it.
translations.literal: But when Loki was loose become, then spoke he, that that gold should become to-them to death, who had [it].

original: Fáfnir ok Reginn beiddu Hreiðmar föður sinn hlutdeildar í gullinu, en hann lézt ekki eitt granahár láta skyldu.

translations.faulkes: Fafnir and Regin asked their father Hreidmar for a share of the gold, but he said he would not give up a single whisker-hair of it.
translations.brodeur: Fáfnir and Reginn asked Hreiðmarr their father for a share of the gold in compensation for their brother; but he would not grant them even a whisker-hair's weight of the gold.
translations.literal: Fafnir and Regin asked Hreidmar father theirs share-part in gold-the, but he said-himself not one whisker-hair let should.

original: Þá drápu þeir bræðr föður sinn. En Fáfnir tók gullit allt ok varð at ormi ok lagðist á Gnitaheiði.

translations.faulkes: Then the brothers killed their father. But Fafnir took all the gold and became a dragon and lay down on Gnita-heath.
translations.brodeur: Then the brothers slew their father for the gold; and Fáfnir took all the gold and became a serpent, and lay him down on Gnita-Heath.
translations.literal: Then slew they brothers father theirs. But Fafnir took gold-the all and became at serpent and laid-himself on Gnita-heath.

original: Reginn fór til Hjálpreks konungs ok varð þar smiðr hans. Hann tók til fóstrs Sigurð, son Sigmundar.

translations.faulkes: Regin went to King Hjalprek and became his smith. He took as his foster-son Sigurd, son of Sigmund.
translations.brodeur: Reginn fled away to King Hjálprekr, and there he became a smith; and he took to foster Sigurðr, son of Sigmundr.
translations.literal: Regin went to Hjalprek king and became there smith his. He took to foster Sigurd, son of-Sigmund.

original: Reginn gerði Sigurði sverð þat, er Gramr heitir, ok var svá hvasst, at Sigurðr brá því í rennanda vatn, ok tók í sundr ullarlagð.

translations.faulkes: Regin made for Sigurd the sword called Gram, and it was so sharp that Sigurd thrust it into running water and it cut through a tuft of wool.
translations.brodeur: Reginn wrought for Sigurðr the sword called Gramr, which was so sharp that when Sigurðr held it in running water, it cut asunder a flock of wool.
translations.literal: Regin made for-Sigurd sword that, which Gram named-is, and was so sharp, that Sigurd cast that in running water, and took in sunder wool-tuft.

original: Sigurðr reið á Gnitaheiði ok drap Fáfni orm í gröf sinni. Þá mælti Reginn, at Sigurðr skyldi steikja hjarta Fáfnis.

translations.faulkes: Sigurd rode to Gnita-heath and killed the dragon Fafnir in his pit. Then Regin said that Sigurd should roast Fafnir's heart.
translations.brodeur: Sigurðr rode up onto Gnita-Heath and slew Fáfnir; and then Reginn bade him roast Fáfnir's heart.
translations.literal: Sigurd rode on Gnita-heath and slew Fafnir serpent in pit his. Then spoke Regin, that Sigurd should roast heart of-Fafnir.

original: En er Sigurðr tók fingri á hjartanu ok vildi vita, ef steikt væri, þá brann hann ok drap fingrinum á tungu sér.

translations.faulkes: And when Sigurd touched the heart with his finger and wanted to know if it was cooked, he burned himself and put his finger to his tongue.
translations.brodeur: But when Sigurðr touched the heart with his finger to see if it were roasted, he burned his finger, and laid it in his mouth.
translations.literal: But when Sigurd took finger on heart-the and wished know, if roasted were, then burned he and dropped finger-the on tongue his.

original: Þá nam hann fugls rödd ok skildi, hvat fuglar sögðu, at Reginn vildi drepa hann. Þá drap hann Regin ok tók gullit.

translations.faulkes: Then he understood the voice of birds and understood what the birds were saying, that Regin wanted to kill him. Then he killed Regin and took the gold.
translations.brodeur: When the heart's blood came upon his tongue, he understood the song of birds, and knew what they said: that Reginn intended to slay him. Then he slew Reginn and took the gold.
translations.literal: Then took he bird's voice and understood, what birds said, that Regin wished slay him. Then slew he Regin and took gold-the.

original: Sigurðr reið upp á Hindarfjall ok kom þar í skjaldborg ok fann þar konu sofanda með hjálmi ok brynju.

translations.faulkes: Sigurd rode up onto Hindarfjall and came to a ring of shields and found a woman sleeping there with a helmet and mail-shirt.
translations.brodeur: Sigurðr rode up onto Hindarfjall and came thither to a shield-burg, and found there a woman sleeping, in helm and birnie.
translations.literal: Sigurd rode up on Hindarfjall and came there in shield-burg and found there woman sleeping with helmet and byrnie.

original: Hón nefndist Brynhildr ok var valkyrja. Sigurðr reið síðan til Gjúka konungs ok gekk í fóstbræðralag við sonu hans.

translations.faulkes: She was called Brynhild and was a valkyrie. Sigurd then rode to King Gjuki and entered into blood-brotherhood with his sons.
translations.brodeur: She was named Brynhildr, and was a Valkyrie. Sigurðr rode thence to King Gjúki, and was made his sworn-brother.
translations.literal: She named-herself Brynhild and was valkyrie. Sigurd rode since to Gjuki king and went in foster-brotherhood with sons his.

original: Sigurðr fékk Guðrúnar dóttur Gjúka. En Gunnar Gjúkason vildi eiga Brynhildi.

translations.faulkes: Sigurd married Gudrun, daughter of Gjuki. But Gunnar Gjúkason wanted to marry Brynhild.
translations.brodeur: Sigurðr wedded Guðrún, Gjúki's daughter; and Gunnar Gjúkason would have Brynhildr.
translations.literal: Sigurd got [married] Gudrun daughter of-Gjuki. But Gunnar Gjúkason wished have [marry] Brynhild.

original: Sigurðr brá sér í Gunnarshams líki ok reið í gegnum eldinn ok fastnaði Brynhildi.

translations.faulkes: Sigurd changed into Gunnar's form and rode through the fire and betrothed Brynhild.
translations.brodeur: Sigurðr changed shapes with Gunnar, and rode through the fire to Brynhildr; and he betrothed her to Gunnar.
translations.literal: Sigurd cast himself in Gunnar's-hide likeness and rode in through fire-the and betrothed Brynhild.

original: En er Brynhildr ok Guðrún deildu á á, þá sýndi Guðrún henni hringinn Andvaranaut ok sagði, at Sigurðr hafði tekit hann af henni.

translations.faulkes: But when Brynhild and Gudrun quarreled at the river, Gudrun showed her the ring Andvaranaut and said that Sigurd had taken it from her.
translations.brodeur: When Brynhildr and Guðrún went into the water to wash their hair, Guðrún showed Brynhildr the ring Andvaranaut, and said that Sigurðr had given it to her.
translations.literal: But when Brynhild and Gudrun disputed at river, then showed Gudrun her ring-the Andvaranaut and said, that Sigurd had taken him from her.

original: Brynhildr eggjaði Gunnar at drepa Sigurð. Guttormr bróðir Gunnars drap Sigurð sofanda.

translations.faulkes: Brynhild incited Gunnar to kill Sigurd. Guttorm, Gunnar's brother, killed Sigurd while he was sleeping.
translations.brodeur: Brynhildr egged on Gunnar to slay Sigurðr; and Guttormr, Gunnar's brother, slew Sigurðr as he slept.
translations.literal: Brynhild egged Gunnar to slay Sigurd. Guttorm brother of-Gunnar slew Sigurd sleeping.

original: Þá drap Brynhildr sjálfa sig, en Gunnar ok Högni tóku gullit allt.

translations.faulkes: Then Brynhild killed herself, and Gunnar and Hogni took all the gold.
translations.brodeur: Then Brynhildr slew herself; and Gunnar and Högni took all the gold.
translations.literal: Then slew Brynhild herself-self, but Gunnar and Hogni took gold-the all.

original: Guðrún fékk Atla konungs, bróður Brynhildar. Hann bauð Gunnari ok Högna til sín ok vildi ná gullinu.

translations.faulkes: Gudrun married King Atli, Brynhild's brother. He invited Gunnar and Hogni to his home and wanted to get the gold.
translations.brodeur: Guðrún was wedded to King Atli, Brynhildr's brother. Atli invited Gunnar and Högni to him, and they went; but he would have their gold.
translations.literal: Gudrun got Atli king, brother of-Brynhild. He invited Gunnar and Hogni to himself and wished reach gold-the.

original: Atli lét skera hjarta ór Högna, en Gunnar var kastaðr í ormgarð. Hann lék horpu með fótunum, þar til er ormr einn hjó hann til hjarta.

translations.faulkes: Atli had the heart cut out of Hogni, and Gunnar was thrown into a snake-pit. He played the harp with his feet until a snake bit him to the heart.
translations.brodeur: Atli let cut the heart out of Högni; but Gunnar he let cast into a snake-pit. There Gunnar played the harp with his toes, until a serpent struck him to the heart.
translations.literal: Atli let cut heart from Hogni, but Gunnar was thrown in serpent-yard. He played harp with feet-the, there until serpent one hewed him to heart.

original: Guðrún drap Atla konung ok sonu hans tvo ok gaf honum at eta hjörtu þeira.

translations.faulkes: Gudrun killed King Atli and his two sons and gave him their hearts to eat.
translations.brodeur: Guðrún slew Atli the King and his two sons, and gave him to eat of their hearts.
translations.literal: Gudrun slew Atli king and sons his two and gave him to eat hearts theirs.

original: Guðrún gekk í sjáinn ok vildi týna sér, en hón rak yfir fjörðinn ok kom til Jónakrs konungs.

translations.faulkes: Gudrun went into the sea and wanted to drown herself, but she drifted across the fjord and came to King Jonakr.
translations.brodeur: Guðrún went out into the sea and would have drowned herself, but she drifted over the firth and came to King Jónakr's land.
translations.literal: Gudrun went in sea-the and wished perish herself, but she drifted over fjord-the and came to Jonakr king.

original: Hón átti þrjá sonu með honum: Sörla ok Hamdi ok Erp. Svanhildr dóttir Sigurðar var ok þar.

translations.faulkes: She had three sons with him: Sorli and Hamdir and Erp. Svanhild, daughter of Sigurd, was also there.
translations.brodeur: She had three sons with him: Sörli, Hamdir, and Erp; Svanhildr, Sigurðr's daughter, was also there.
translations.literal: She had three sons with him: Sorli and Hamdir and Erp. Svanhild daughter of-Sigurd was also there.

original: Jörmunrekr konungr enn ríki vildi eiga Svanhildi Sigurðardóttur. Hann sendi Randvé son sinn at biðja hennar.

translations.faulkes: King Jormunrek the Mighty wanted to marry Svanhild, daughter of Sigurd. He sent his son Randver to woo her.
translations.brodeur: King Jörmunrekr the Mighty desired to wed Svanhildr, daughter of Sigurðr; and he sent Randvér his son to woo her.
translations.literal: Jormunrek king the mighty wished have [marry] Svanhild Sigurd's-daughter. He sent Randve son his to ask-for her.

original: Bikki mælti, at Randvér væri betr fallinn at eiga Svanhildi en faðir hans, er gamall var. Þat líkaði þeim vel.

translations.faulkes: Bikki said that Randver was better suited to marry Svanhild than his father, who was old. That pleased them both well.
translations.brodeur: Bikki said that it were more meet that Randvér should have her, since he was young and she also; but Jörmunrekr was old. This counsel pleased the young folk well.
translations.literal: Bikki spoke, that Randve were better suited to have Svanhild than father his, who old was. That liked them well.

original: Þá sagði Bikki konungi, at sonr hans vildi véla hann. Konungr lét taka Randvé ok leiða til gálga.

translations.faulkes: Then Bikki told the king that his son wanted to betray him. The king had Randver seized and led to the gallows.
translations.brodeur: Then Bikki told the king that Randvér intended to betray him; wherefore the king let seize Randvér and lead him to the gallows.
translations.literal: Then said Bikki to-king, that son his wished trick him. King let take Randve and lead to gallows.

original: En Svanhildi lét hann troða undir hrossa fótum. En er hón lauk upp augun, þá þorðu hrossin eigi at troða hana.

translations.faulkes: And he had Svanhild trampled under horses' feet. But when she opened her eyes, the horses did not dare to trample her.
translations.brodeur: And Svanhildr he caused to be trodden under horses' feet; but when she opened her eyes, the horses dared not tread upon her.
translations.literal: But Svanhild let he tread under horses' feet. But when she locked up [opened] eyes-the, then dared horses-the not to tread her.

original: Þá lét Bikki draga belg á höfuð henni, ok var hón þá troðin til bana. En er Guðrún spurði þetta, þá eggjaði hón sonu sína til hefnda.

translations.faulkes: Then Bikki had a bag drawn over her head, and she was then trampled to death. When Gudrun heard this, she incited her sons to revenge.
translations.brodeur: Then Bikki let take a bag and draw it over her head; and then she was trodden to death. When Guðrún heard this, she egged on her sons to vengeance.
translations.literal: Then let Bikki draw bag on head her, and was she then trodden to death. But when Gudrun asked [heard] this, then egged she sons hers to revenges.

original: Hón gaf þeim brynjar svá sterkar, at járn beit ekki á. Hón skipti orðum við þá, hversu þeir skyldu vinna.

translations.faulkes: She gave them mail-coats so strong that iron could not cut them. She discussed with them how they should perform the deed.
translations.brodeur: She gave them mail-shirts so strong that iron could not bite them; and she counselled them how they should perform the slaying.
translations.literal: She gave them byrnies so strong, that iron bit not on. She shifted words with them, how they should work.

original: Á leiðinni mættu þeir Erpi bróður sínum ok spurðu, hvat hann mundi veita þeim. Hann svarar: 'Svá sem hönd veitir fæti.'

translations.faulkes: On the way they met their brother Erp and asked what help he would give them. He answered: 'Just as a hand helps a foot.'
translations.brodeur: On the way they met Erpr their brother, and asked what help he would afford them. He answered: 'Such as the hand gives the foot.'
translations.literal: On way-the met they Erp brother theirs and asked, what he would provide them. He answers: 'So as hand provides foot.'

original: Þeir drápu hann, því at þeim þótti þat engi veizla. En er þeir kómu til Jörmunreks konungs, þá hjó Sörli af honum hendr, en Hamðir fætr.

translations.faulkes: They killed him, because it seemed to them that was no help. But when they came to King Jormunrek, Sorli cut off his hands and Hamdir his feet.
translations.brodeur: They slew him, for it seemed to them no help at all. When they came to King Jörmunrekr, Sörli hewed off his hands, and Hamdir his feet.
translations.literal: They slew him, because to-them seemed that no help. But when they came to Jormunrek king, then hewed Sorli from him hands, but Hamdir feet.

original: Þá mælti Hamðir: 'Af væri nú höfuðit, ef Erpr lifði.' Þá sóttu menn at þeim, en járn beit eigi á þeim.

translations.faulkes: Then Hamdir said: 'The head would be off now if Erp were alive.' Then men attacked them, but iron did not cut them.
translations.brodeur: Then spake Hamdir: 'The head were off now if Erpr were living.' Then men set upon them, but iron would not bite them.
translations.literal: Then spoke Hamdir: 'Off would-be now head-the, if Erp lived.' Then sought men at them, but iron bit not on them.

original: Jörmunrekr kallaði, at menn skyldu berja þá með grjóti, ok varð þat þeim at bana. Fyrir því er gull kallað dreyri eða blóð Sörla ok Hamðis.

translations.faulkes: Jormunrek called out that men should pelt them with stones, and that was the death of them. For this reason gold is called the gore or blood of Sorli and Hamdir.
translations.brodeur: Jörmunrekr cried out that men should pelt them with stones; and even so it was done, and there they died. Therefore gold is called Gore of Sörli and Hamdir.
translations.literal: Jormunrek called, that men should pelt them with stones, and was that to-them to death. Because that is gold called gore or blood of-Sorli and Hamdir.

original: Hví er gull kallað Fróða mjöl?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called Frodi's meal?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called Frodi's Meal?
translations.literal: Why is gold called Frodi's meal?

original: Skjöldr var sonr Óðins, er Skjöldungar eru frá komnir. Hann bjó í Danmörku.

translations.faulkes: Skjold was a son of Odin, from whom the Skjoldungs are descended. He lived in Denmark.
translations.brodeur: Skjöldr was a son of Odin, from whom the Skjöldungar are descended; he dwelt in Denmark.
translations.literal: Skjold was son of-Odin, whom Skjoldungs are from come. He dwelt in Denmark.

original: Friðleifr hét sonr Skjaldar, er konungdóm tók eftir hann. Sonr Friðleifs hét Fróði.

translations.faulkes: Fridleif was the name of Skjold's son, who took the kingship after him. Fridleif's son was called Frodi.
translations.brodeur: Friðleifr was the name of Skjöldr's son, who took the kingdom after him; Friðleifr's son was Fróði.
translations.literal: Fridleif named-was son of-Skjold, who kingdom took after him. Son of-Fridleif named-was Frodi.

original: Fróði konungr keypti ambáttir tvær, er hétu Fenja ok Menja. Þær váru miklar ok sterkar.

translations.faulkes: King Frodi bought two slave-women called Fenja and Menja. They were large and strong.
translations.brodeur: King Fróði bought two bond-maids, called Fenja and Menja; they were great and strong.
translations.literal: Frodi king bought slave-women two, who named-were Fenja and Menja. They were great and strong.

original: Í Danmörku váru kvernsteinar tveir svá miklir, at engi maðr var svá sterkr, at dregit gæti. Kvernin hét Grotti.

translations.faulkes: In Denmark there were two millstones so large that no man was strong enough to turn them. The mill was called Grotti.
translations.brodeur: In Denmark were two mill-stones so great that no man was so strong that he could turn them. The mill was called Grotti.
translations.literal: In Denmark were millstones two so great, that no man was so strong, that dragged might-have. Mill-the named-was Grotti.

original: Fróði konungr lét fylgja Fenju ok Menju til kvernarinnar ok bað þær mala gull ok frið ok sælu Fróða.

translations.faulkes: King Frodi had Fenja and Menja led to the mill and told them to grind out gold and peace and prosperity for Frodi.
translations.brodeur: King Fróði caused Fenja and Menja to be led to the mill, and bade them grind gold and peace and happiness for Fróði.
translations.literal: Frodi king let follow Fenja and Menja to mill-the and asked them grind gold and peace and happiness Frodi's.

original: Hann gaf þeim eigi lengri hvíld en gaukrinn þagði eða hljóð mátti kveða.

translations.faulkes: He gave them no longer rest than the cuckoo was silent or a song could be sung.
translations.brodeur: He gave them no longer rest than the cuckoo's silence, or the time it took to sing a song.
translations.literal: He gave them not longer rest than cuckoo-the silent-was or sound might recite.

original: Þá mældu þær her á hendr Fróða, ok kom á þeiri nóttu sækonungr sá, er Mýsingr hét, ok drap Fróða.

translations.faulkes: Then they ground out an army against Frodi, and that night there came a sea-king called Mysing and killed Frodi.
translations.brodeur: Then they ground out an army against Fróði; and that night came a sea-king called Mýsingr and slew Fróði.
translations.literal: Then ground they army on hands Frodi's, and came on that night sea-king that, who Mysing named-was, and slew Frodi.

original: Mýsingr tók Grotta ok ambáttirnar ok flutti á skip sín. Hann bað þær mala salt.

translations.faulkes: Mysing took Grotti and the slave-women and carried them to his ships. He told them to grind salt.
translations.brodeur: Mýsingr took Grotti and the bond-maids and carried them to his ships. He bade them grind salt.
translations.literal: Mysing took Grotti and slave-women-the and moved on ships his. He asked them grind salt.

original: At miðri nótt spurðu þær, ef eigi væri salt nóg. Hann bað þær mala lengr. Skömmu síðar sökk skipið, ok varð þar særinn saltr.

translations.faulkes: At midnight they asked if there was not enough salt. He told them to grind longer. A short time later the ship sank, and the sea became salt there.
translations.brodeur: At midnight they asked if they had ground salt enough. He bade them grind longer. A little space thereafter the ship sank; and there the sea became salt.
translations.literal: At middle night asked they, if not were salt enough. He asked them grind longer. Short-time after sank ship-the, and became there sea-the salt.

original: Hví er gull kallað dagsbrá eða dagsgerði dverganna?

translations.faulkes: Why is gold called the dwarf's daybreak or the dwarf's daylight?
translations.brodeur: Why is gold called the Day-Spring of the Dwarfs?
translations.literal: Why is gold called day-brow or day-enclosure of-dwarfs-the?

original: Því at þeir búa í steinum ok dvergar þola eigi dagsbrún, en gullit lýsir svá í steinum sem dagsbrún á himni.

translations.faulkes: Because they live in rocks and dwarfs cannot endure the daybreak, but gold shines in the rocks just like the dawn in the sky.
translations.brodeur: Because they dwell in rocks, and dwarfs cannot endure the daybreak; but gold shines in the rocks even as the dawn in the sky.
translations.literal: Because that they dwell in stones and dwarfs endure not day-brow, but gold-the shines so in stones as day-brow on heaven.

original: Hvernig skal kenna mann? Svá, at kalla hann verki sínum eða því, er hann gefr eða þiggjr.

translations.faulkes: How should man be referred to? By calling him by his work or by what he gives or receives.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should a man be periphrased? Thus: by his works, or by that which he gives or receives.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] man? So, that call him work his or that, which he gives or receives.

original: Ok kalla hann viðu eða tré eða lund, en kenna við vopn eða skip eða við auð sinn.

translations.faulkes: And call him trees or wood or grove, and describe him in terms of weapons or ships or his wealth.
translations.brodeur: And calling him wood or tree, and periphrasing him in terms of weapons or of ships or of his wealth.
translations.literal: And call him wood or tree or grove, but ken [describe] with weapons or ships or with wealth his.

original: Hví er maðr kallaðr tré?

translations.faulkes: Why is man called a tree?
translations.brodeur: Why is a man called a tree?
translations.literal: Why is man called tree?

original: Því at hinir fyrstu menn, Askur ok Embla, váru sköpuð ór trjám, Askur ór aski en Embla ór álmi.

translations.faulkes: Because the first people, Ask and Embla, were shaped from trees, Ask from an ash and Embla from an elm.
translations.brodeur: Because the first men, Askur and Embla, were created out of trees: Askur out of an ash, and Embla out of an elm.
translations.literal: Because that those first men, Ask and Embla, were shaped from trees, Ask from ash but Embla from elm.

original: Hvernig skal kenna konu? Svá, at kalla hana viðum eða trjám ok kenna við gull eða steina eða dýran búnað.

translations.faulkes: How should woman be referred to? By calling her trees or wood and describing her in terms of gold or gems or costly attire.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should a woman be periphrased? Thus: by calling her by the names of trees, and periphrasing her in terms of gold or of gems or of precious raiment.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] woman? So, that call her woods or trees and ken [describe] with gold or stones or dear [precious] attire.

original: Ok kalla hana selju eða lund eða eik eða hlyn, en kenna við drykk eða borð eða horn.

translations.faulkes: And call her willow or grove or oak or maple, and describe her in terms of drink or tables or horns.
translations.brodeur: And calling her willow or grove or oak or maple, and periphrasing her in terms of drink or of the board or of the horn.
translations.literal: And call her willow or grove or oak or maple, but ken [describe] with drink or table or horn.

original: Hví er kona kölluð eik?

translations.faulkes: Why is woman called an oak?
translations.brodeur: Why is a woman called an oak?
translations.literal: Why is woman called oak?

original: Því at hón er kallað þáttur eða grein þeira kvenna, er gert höfðu stórvirki.

translations.faulkes: Because she is called a branch or a bough of those women who had performed great deeds.
translations.brodeur: Because she is called the part or the branch of those women who have done great deeds.
translations.literal: Because that she is called part or branch of-those women, who had made great-works.

original: Hvernig skal kenna skip? Svá, at kalla þat hest eða dýr eða fugl sjávar.

translations.faulkes: How should a ship be referred to? By calling it a horse or animal or bird of the sea.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should a ship be periphrased? Thus: by calling it horse, or beast, or bird of the sea.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] ship? So, that call it horse or beast or bird of-sea.

original: Ok kenna við vinda eða við rár eða við segl eða við skrið sinn.

translations.faulkes: And describe it in terms of winds or yards or sails or its motion.
translations.brodeur: And periphrasing it in terms of the winds, or of the yards, or of the sails, or of its course.
translations.literal: And ken [describe] with winds or with yards or with sails or with motion its.

original: Hvernig skal kenna sjá? Svá, at kalla hann land dverganna eða hús Ægis.

translations.faulkes: How should the sea be referred to? By calling it the land of the dwarfs or Aegir's house.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the sea be periphrased? Thus: by calling it the Land of the Dwarfs, or the House of Ægir.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] sea? So, that call it land of-dwarfs-the or house of-Aegir.

original: Ok kalla hann veg eða braut skipanna eða dreyra jarðar.

translations.faulkes: And call it the path or road of ships or the blood of the earth.
translations.brodeur: And calling it the Road or the Path of Ships, or the Blood of the Earth.
translations.literal: And call it way or path of-ships-the or blood of-earth.

original: Hví er sjárinn kallaðr dreyri jarðar?

translations.faulkes: Why is the sea called the blood of the earth?
translations.brodeur: Why is the sea called the Blood of the Earth?
translations.literal: Why is sea-the called blood of-earth?

original: Því at hann var gerr ór blóði Ymis jötuns, er goðin drápu hann.

translations.faulkes: Because it was made from the blood of the giant Ymir when the gods killed him.
translations.brodeur: Because it was created out of the blood of the giant Ymir, when the gods slew him.
translations.literal: Because that it was made from blood of-Ymir giant, when gods-the slew him.

original: Hvernig skal kenna himin? Svá, at kalla hann hús eða sal vindsins eða land goðanna.

translations.faulkes: How should the sky be referred to? By calling it the house or hall of the wind or the land of the gods.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the heavens be periphrased? Thus: by calling them the House or the Hall of the Wind, or the Land of the Gods.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] heaven? So, that call it house or hall of-wind-the or land of-gods-the.

original: Ok kalla hann hjálm eða skýli jarðar eða byrði dverganna.

translations.faulkes: And call it the helmet or covering of the earth or the burden of the dwarfs.
translations.brodeur: And calling them the Helm or the Cover of the Earth, or the Burden of the Dwarfs.
translations.literal: And call it helmet or covering of-earth or burden of-dwarfs-the.

original: Hví er himinn kallaðr byrði dverganna?

translations.faulkes: Why is the sky called the burden of the dwarfs?
translations.brodeur: Why are the heavens called the Burden of the Dwarfs?
translations.literal: Why is heaven called burden of-dwarfs-the?

original: Því at dvergarnir fjórir, Austri ok Vestri, Norðri ok Suðri, halda honum upp.

translations.faulkes: Because the four dwarfs, Austri and Vestri, Nordri and Sudri, hold it up.
translations.brodeur: Because the four dwarfs, Austri, Vestri, Norðri, and Suðri, hold them up.
translations.literal: Because that dwarfs-the four, Austri and Vestri, Nordri and Sudri, hold him [it] up.

original: Hvernig skal kenna jörð? Svá, at kalla hana hold Ymis ok móður Þórs, dóttur Ónars, brúði Óðins.

translations.faulkes: How should earth be referred to? By calling her Ymir's flesh and mother of Thor, daughter of Onar, bride of Odin.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the earth be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Ymir's Flesh, and Mother of Thor, Daughter of Ónarr, Bride of Odin.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] earth? So, that call her flesh of-Ymir and mother of-Thor, daughter of-Onar, bride of-Odin.

original: Ok kalla hana eldshús vindsins eða skip dverganna eða hold hins forna jötuns.

translations.faulkes: And call her the fire-house of the wind or the ship of the dwarfs or the flesh of the ancient giant.
translations.brodeur: And calling her the Fire-House of the Wind, or the Ship of the Dwarfs, or the Flesh of the Ancient Giant.
translations.literal: And call her fire-house of-wind-the or ship of-dwarfs-the or flesh of-the ancient giant.

original: Hví er jörð kölluð hold Ymis?

translations.faulkes: Why is earth called Ymir's flesh?
translations.brodeur: Why is the earth called Ymir's Flesh?
translations.literal: Why is earth called flesh of-Ymir?

original: Því at goðin sköpuðu hana ór holdi hans, er þeir drápu hann í upphafi.

translations.faulkes: Because the gods created it from his flesh when they killed him in the beginning.
translations.brodeur: Because the gods created the earth out of his flesh, when they slew him in the beginning.
translations.literal: Because that gods-the shaped her from flesh his, when they slew him in beginning.

original: Hvernig skal kenna sól? Svá, at kalla hana dóttur Mundilfara, systur mána, eldi himins ok jarðar.

translations.faulkes: How should the sun be referred to? By calling her daughter of Mundilfari, sister of the moon, fire of heaven and earth.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the sun be periphrased? Thus: by calling her Daughter of Mundilfari, Sister of the Moon, Fire of Heaven and Earth.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] sun? So, that call her daughter of-Mundilfari, sister of-moon, fire of-heaven and earth.

original: Hvernig skal kenna mána? Svá, at kalla hann son Mundilfara, bróður sólar, taltól ársins.

translations.faulkes: How should the moon be referred to? By calling him son of Mundilfari, brother of the sun, counter of the year.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the moon be periphrased? Thus: by calling him Son of Mundilfari, Brother of the Sun, Counter of the Year.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] moon? So, that call him son of-Mundilfari, brother of-sun, counting-tool of-year-the.

original: Hvernig skal kenna eld? Svá, at kalla hann bróður vindsins ok sjávar, bana viðarins eða húsanna.

translations.faulkes: How should fire be referred to? By calling it brother of the wind and sea, destroyer of wood or of houses.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should fire be periphrased? Thus: by calling it Brother of the Wind and of the Sea, Slayer of Wood and of Houses.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] fire? So, that call him brother of-wind-the and sea, slayer of-wood-the or of-houses-the.

original: Hví er eldr kallaðr bróðir vindsins?

translations.faulkes: Why is fire called brother of the wind?
translations.brodeur: Why is fire called Brother of the Wind?
translations.literal: Why is fire called brother of-wind-the?

original: Því at Fornjótr átti þrjá sonu: einn var Ægir, annarr Logi, þriði Kári. Logi er eldr, en Kári er vindr.

translations.faulkes: Because Fornjot had three sons: one was Aegir, another Logi, the third Kari. Logi is fire, and Kari is wind.
translations.brodeur: Because Fornjótr had three sons: one was Ægir, the second Logi, the third Kári... Logi is fire, and Kári is wind.
translations.literal: Because that Fornjot had three sons: one was Aegir, second Logi, third Kari. Logi is fire, but Kari is wind.

original: Hvernig skal kenna vind? Svá, at kalla hann son Fornjóts, bróður Ægis ok Loga, hund skýja.

translations.faulkes: How should the wind be referred to? By calling it son of Fornjot, brother of Aegir and Logi, the dog of clouds.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should the wind be periphrased? Thus: by calling it Son of Fornjótr, Brother of Ægir and of Logi, Hound of Clouds.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] wind? So, that call him son of-Fornjot, brother of-Aegir and Logi, dog of-clouds.

original: Hvernig skal kenna sumar? Svá, at kalla þat son Svásuðar, hvíld snjávar, vöxt jarðar.

translations.faulkes: How should summer be referred to? By calling it son of Svasud, rest of the snow, growth of the earth.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should summer be periphrased? Thus: by calling it Son of Svásuðr, the Ease of the Snow, the Growth of the Earth.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] summer? So, that call that son of-Svasud, rest of-snow, growth of-earth.

original: Hvernig skal kenna vetr? Svá, at kalla hann son Vindsvals, bana blóma, hlekk skipanna.

translations.faulkes: How should winter be referred to? By calling it son of Vindsval, destroyer of blossoms, shackle of ships.
translations.brodeur: In what wise should winter be periphrased? Thus: by calling it Son of Vindsvalr, Slayer of Blossoms, the Shackle of Ships.
translations.literal: How shall ken [describe] winter? So, that call him son of-Vindsval, slayer of-blossoms, shackle of-ships-the.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á konungum? Siklingr, lofðungr, fylkir, hilmir, ræsir.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for kings? Sikling, lofdung, fylkir, hilmir, raesir.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for kings? Siklingr, Lofðungr, Fylkir, Hilmir, Ræsir.
translations.literal: What names are on kings? Sikling, lofdung, fylkir, hilmir, raesir.

original: Ok kalla þá buðlunga, víðreka, skjöldunga, ynglinga.

translations.faulkes: And call them budlungs, wide-rulers, skjoldungs, ynglings.
translations.brodeur: And calling them Buðlungar, Wide-rulers, Skjöldungar, Ynglingar.
translations.literal: And call them budlungs, wide-drivers, skjoldungs, ynglings.

original: Hví eru konungar kallaðir skjöldungar?

translations.faulkes: Why are kings called skjoldungs?
translations.brodeur: Why are kings called Skjöldungar?
translations.literal: Why are kings called skjoldungs?

original: Því at þeir eru komnir frá Skjöldi, syni Óðins, er bjó í Danmörku.

translations.faulkes: Because they are descended from Skjold, son of Odin, who lived in Denmark.
translations.brodeur: Because they are descended from Skjöldr, son of Odin, who dwelt in Denmark.
translations.literal: Because that they are come from Skjold, son of-Odin, who dwelt in Denmark.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á her ok fólki? Lýðr, þjóð, skarri, drengir, kempur.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for a host and for the common people? Lýðr, Þjóð, Skarri, Drengir, Kempur.
translations.literal: What names are on army and folk? Lyth, thjod, skarri, drengs, kemps.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á orrostum? Hjörþing, skjaldmót, vápnagöll, róg.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for battles? Sword-Thing, Shield-Meeting, Weapon-Clash, Strife.
translations.literal: What names are on battles? Sword-thing, shield-meeting, weapon-clash, strife.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á sverðum? Hjörr, mækir, brandr, egg, sax.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for swords? Hjörr, Mækir, Brandr, Egg, Sax.
translations.literal: What names are on swords? Hjorr, maekir, brand, edge, sax.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for spears? Geirr, Darraðr, Skeggja, Frakka, Dörr.
translations.literal: What names are on spears? Geir, darrad, skeggja, frakka, dorr.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for helmets? Hilmir, Hróðr, Hylr, Skyggnir, Gríma.
translations.literal: What names are on helmets? Hilmir, hrodr, hylr, skyggnir, grima.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for byrnies? Hamr, Skikkja, Taug, Hringnet, Hlíð.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for arrows? Skutill, Skot, Flaug, Þrefingr, Broddr.
translations.literal: What names are on arrows? Skutill, shot, flight, threfing, spike.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á bogum? Ýr, álmbogi, tvitól, skotvápn.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for bows? Ýr, Álm-bow, Tvitól, Shooting-weapon.
translations.literal: What names are on bows? Yew, elm-bow, double-tool, shooting-weapon.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á skipum? Askur, knörr, langskip, karfi, snekkja.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for ships? Askur, Knörr, Langskip, Karfi, Snekkja.
translations.literal: What names are on ships? Ash, knorr, longship, karve, snekke.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for parts of a ship? Húfr, Mastr, Rár, Segl, Stýri, Árar.

translations.literal: What names are on heaven? Heidthornir, loft, wide-strider, ring-the, flash.

translations.literal: What names are on sun and moon? All-white, Dvalin's plaything, tool of-year-the.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á sjó? Haf, marr, ægir, vágur, straumr, dýpi.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for the sea? Haf, Marr, Ægir, Vágur, Straumr, Dýpi.
translations.literal: What names are on sea? Ocean, sea, aegir, wave, current, depth.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á eldi ok vindi? Logi, hyrr, gapi, þytur, gustur.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for fire and wind? Logi, Hyrr, Gapi, Þytur, Gustur.
translations.literal: What names are on fire and wind? Flame, embers, gaper, noise, gust.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á vörgum? Fenrir, Garmr, freki, vargur, úlfur.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for wolves? Fenrir, Garmr, Freki, Vargur, Úlfur.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for bears? Björn, Jötunn, Hrammi, Dýr, Hvítabjörn.
translations.literal: What names are on bears? Bear, giant, paw-one, beast, white-bear.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for deer? Hart, beast, horned-one, swift-one, wood-beast.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for harts? Hjörtr, Dýr, Horni, Hraði, Skógardýr.
translations.literal: What names are on deer? Hart, beast, horned, swift, forest-beast.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á hestum? Sleipnir, Gulltoppr, jó, fákur, vigg.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for horses? Sleipnir, Gulltopp, steed, mount, war-horse.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for horses? Sleipnir, Gulltoppr, Jó, Fákur, Vigg.
translations.literal: What names are on horses? Sleipnir, Gulltopp, horse, mount, war-horse.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á uxum? Uxi, naut, tarfur, gapi, maugur.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for oxen? Ox, cattle, bull, gaper, youth.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for oxen? Uxi, Naut, Tarfur, Gapi, Maugur.
translations.literal: What names are on oxen? Ox, cattle, bull, gaper, youth.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á örnum? Örn, ari, huginni, flaug, fjöðr.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for eagles? Eagle, ari, thought-one, flight, feather.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for eagles? Örn, Ari, Huginni, Flaug, Fjöðr.
translations.literal: What names are on eagles? Eagle, ari, hugin-like, flight, feather.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for ravens? Hrafn, Huginn, Muninn, Korpur, Muninn.
translations.literal: What names are on ravens? Raven, Hugin, Munin, crow, memory-one.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for cocks and hens? Hani, Hæna, Galinn, Dvergi, Fugl.
translations.literal: What names are on cocks and hens? Cock, hen, crowing-one, dwarf-bird, bird.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for fish? Fiskr, Lax, Hvalr, Gedda, Þorskur.
translations.literal: What names are on fish? Fish, salmon, whale, pike, cod.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á ormum? Ormr, dreki, fafnir, naðr, jörmungandr.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for serpents? Serpent, dragon, fafnir, adder, midgard-serpent.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for serpents? Ormr, Dreki, Fáfnir, Naðr, Jörmungandr.
translations.literal: What names are on serpents? Serpent, dragon, fafnir, adder, earth-wand [Jormungandr].

original: Hvaða heiti eru á höfði? Höfuð, kalla, gapi, hjálm-staðr, skel.

translations.faulkes: What names are there for the head? Head, cranium, gaper, helmet-place, shell.
translations.brodeur: What names are there for the head? Höfuð, Kalla, Gapi, Helm-stead, Skel.
translations.literal: What names are on head? Head, cranium, gaper, helmet-stead, shell.

original: Hvaða heiti eru á hári? Haddur, hár, lá, skógur höfuðsins, fjaðrar.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for hair? Haddur, Hár, Lá, Forest of the Head, Feathers.
translations.literal: What names are on hair? Tresses, hair, locks, forest of-head-the, feathers.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for ears? Eyra, Hlust, Tool of Hearing, Gátt.
translations.literal: What names are on ears? Ear, hearing-hole, hearing-tool, gate.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for the nose? Nef, Rani, Snjáldur, Tool of Scent.
translations.literal: What names are on nose? Nose, snout, muzzle, scent-tool.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for the mouth? Munnur, Kjaftur, House of Speech, House of Words.
translations.literal: What names are on mouth? Mouth, jaws, speech-house, word-house.

translations.literal: What names are on tongue? Tongue, speech-nail, discourse-ski, word-stick.

translations.brodeur: What names are there for teeth? Tennur, Straw-saws, Word-fence, Rocks of the Mouth.
translations.literal: What names are on teeth? Teeth, straw-saws, word-yard [fence], stones of-mouth-the.
